syllabus_desc: Describe the basic characteristics of High Frequency (HF) and Very High Frequency (VHF) in terms of: Range
concept: Right, so here's the deal with HF and VHF range. Your VHF radio works on line-of-sight—if you can't see the horizon from where you are, the signal won't reach it either. The higher you climb, the further you can talk. There's actually a rough formula you might see: about 1.23 times the square root of your altitude in feet gives you your range in nautical miles to the horizon. So at 5,000 feet, you're looking at roughly 85 NM. Now HF is totally different. It bounces off the ionosphere—we call that sky wave propagation—which is why you can talk across oceans and to the other side of the world. That's why Airways NZ uses HF when you're crossing the Tasman. The catch? HF's a bit trickier to use, and the signal quality depends on the time of day and solar activity. For your local flying around Ardmore or Hamilton, VHF does the job just fine.
exam_tip: VHF is line-of-sight (range ≈ 1.23√alt ft); HF bounces off ionosphere for long-range comms.
key_facts: VHF range increases with altitude; horizon distance formula: 1.23 × √height(feet) = NM
VHF requires line-of-sight propagation; signal blocked by terrain and curvature
HF uses sky wave propagation (ionospheric reflection) for oceanic and long-distance comms
HF range independent of altitude but affected by time of day and solar activity
NZ aviation VHF band: 118.000–136.975 MHz; HF: 3–30 MHz
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Describe the basic characteristics of High Frequency (HF) and Very High Frequency (VHF) in terms of: Surface interference
concept: VHF's pretty clean in most cases, but you can pick up static if there's a lot of electrical activity—thunderstorms especially. The signal bounces around a bit if you're near mountains or built-up areas, so you might get some multipath distortion, but generally VHF stays pretty clear. HF, on the other hand, can be a real pain for surface interference. Because it's bouncing off the ionosphere, you can get interference from radio stations, even from other parts of the world using the same frequency. You'll also hear background noise and fading. That's why HF gets noisier at certain times of day. When you're flying the Tasman crossing and working on HF with Airways, you'll notice the signal fades in and out. If you're crossing to Auckland from Wellington on a winter morning, you might get better HF propagation than on a summer evening—it's all about what the ionosphere's doing.
exam_tip: VHF: relatively clear, affected by storms and terrain; HF: noisy, fading, affected by time of day.
key_facts: VHF surface interference minimal; electrical activity and thunderstorms cause static
VHF multipath distortion possible near terrain and built-up areas
HF surface interference significant; other radio stations and distant signals interfere
HF signal fading and noise varies with time of day and solar cycle
HF propagation degrades during daylight hours on some paths; improves at night on others
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Describe the basic characteristics of High Frequency (HF) and Very High Frequency (VHF) in terms of: Clarity of reception
concept: VHF reception is crisp and clear—that's one of the big advantages. You get good fidelity, so voices come through cleanly. You can hear everything clearly as long as you're in range and you've got line-of-sight. That's why it's perfect for chatting to Christchurch Information on 119.1 MHz or your local tower. HF clarity is a different story altogether. You'll get background noise, sometimes quite a bit of it, especially on longer paths. The signal might fade in and out, which makes it harder to understand. Sometimes you'll get distortion or a slight 'hollow' sound. That's normal for HF—it's just the nature of bouncing the signal off the ionosphere over long distances. When you're doing a radio check on HF, you might only understand every other word until you both settle in and you learn to key the mike more carefully. Don't be surprised if Airways asks you to repeat things on HF—it doesn't mean your English is bad!
exam_tip: VHF: clear, high fidelity; HF: noisy, fading, lower clarity—especially long-distance.
key_facts: VHF reception clear and high-fidelity within line-of-sight range
VHF clarity degraded by atmospheric noise, static from electrical activity, terrain blocking
HF reception typically includes background noise and variable signal clarity
HF signal fading and multipath cause distortion and reduced intelligibility on long paths
HF clarity improves with careful microphone technique and slower speech rate
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: VHF radio controls: volume, squelch, frequency selection, PTT
concept: Your radio's got four main controls you need to know. Volume—that's straightforward, turn it up to hear and down if it's blasting. Squelch mutes the background hiss when no one's transmitting. Turn it up too far and you'll miss weak signals; too low and all you hear is static. Get it set right for your environment—usually somewhere in the middle. Frequency selection: you'll use the number pad or a knob to dial in your frequency. In NZ, we use 118.000 to 136.975 MHz on 25 kHz spacing. Double-check your frequency before you transmit—you don't want to call up Christchurch tower thinking you're on the AFIS frequency! The PTT (push-to-talk) button is your transmit switch. Press it to talk, release to listen. Simple, but you'd be amazed how many people forget to press it fully or lean on it while they're thinking about what to say. One last thing: get familiar with your specific radio. Every aircraft's radio is slightly different, and you need to know where these controls are without looking.
exam_tip: Master volume, squelch (set to avoid static without cutting weak signals), frequency entry, and PTT technique.
key_facts: Volume: controls speaker output; squelch: suppresses background noise when no signal present
Squelch setting critical—too high misses weak signals; too low leaves hissing noise
Frequency selection: NZ VHF band 118.000–136.975 MHz on 25 kHz spacing; verify before transmit
PTT (push-to-talk): press fully and hold while speaking; release to receive
Know your radio's layout and control positions by feel; don't search during flight
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Intercom systems and audio selector panels
concept: Most aircraft have an intercom so you and your passengers can talk. There's usually a panel with switches to select who's transmitting and who's receiving. You might have channels for the pilot, co-pilot, cabin, or different crew positions. Learn which switch position lets you talk to the cabin when the comms are on, and which one isolates the flight deck for ATC conversations. Audio selector panels let you choose what's going into your headset—radio 1, radio 2, navigation aid audio, or a combination. If you've got two radios and you're on approach, you might have radio 1 going to your headset and radio 2 on standby. The key is not to get confused mid-flight. Set it up before you're busy, and don't fiddle with it during critical phases. Some aircraft have automatic selection—when you key the mike on radio 1, the audio automatically switches. Familiarize yourself with your aircraft's setup during preflight, and if you're not sure, ask another pilot or check the AFM.
exam_tip: Know your intercom routing and audio selector settings; don't adjust during critical phases.
key_facts: Intercom switches select who transmits and receives (pilot, co-pilot, cabin, crew positions)
Audio selector panels route audio to headset from different sources (radio 1/2, nav audio, etc.)
Pre-set audio routing before flight; familiarize with automatic switching if equipped
Understand isolation procedures to prevent cabin/passengers overheating flight deck comms
Check aircraft AFM for specific intercom and audio panel configuration and operation
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Headset and microphone use
concept: Your headset and microphone are your connection to the world outside. The headset's got earpieces for both ears so you hear in stereo and can pick out radio calls even in a noisy cockpit. The boom microphone hangs in front of your mouth, usually adjustable so you can position it about 2 or 3 centimeters away from your lips. Too close and you'll get plosive pops; too far and you're quiet. The microphone's got a PTT switch on the boom—most of the time that's what you're pressing, not a panel switch. Some aircraft use a hand-held mic instead, which you hold and press the transmit button. With a boom mic, you can keep both hands free for flying. Make sure your headset's comfortable because you'll be wearing it for hours. Check the audio connections before flight—a loose plug and you won't hear anything. Test your transmission too; ask ATC to give you a radio check so you know you're being heard. Don't assume your headset's working just because you can hear; you need to confirm you can transmit too.
exam_tip: Boom mic 2–3 cm from lips; check audio connections; confirm both Rx and Tx during radio check.
key_facts: Headset: binaural earpieces for stereo hearing; boom or hand-held microphone options
Boom microphone positioning: 2–3 cm from lips; prevents plosives and ensures good audio quality
Boom mic allows hands-free operation; hand-held mic requires holding and PTT button press
Audio connections must be secure; loose connectors cause reception failure or dead transmit
Test transmission during radio check to confirm both receive and transmit functioning
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Describe the correct operation of: The headset/boom microphone combination
concept: Using a boom mic correctly makes you sound professional and ensures everyone hears you. First, put on your headset snugly but comfortably—you're going to wear it for a while. Adjust the boom so the microphone is about a thumbs-width away from your mouth, pointing slightly downward. When you speak, speak clearly and naturally; you don't need to shout. Enunciate your words, especially consonants—that's what people are straining to hear on HF. Press the PTT button firmly and hold it for about a quarter-second before you start talking. This gives the transmitter time to key up. Otherwise, your first syllable gets cut off and people miss the start of your callsign. When you're done talking, release the PTT. Some crews use the 'hand over the boom' technique—hold your hand near the boom so you feel the vibration and know you're transmitting. That's a good habit. If you're in a loud cockpit, pull the boom a tiny bit closer for better clarity, but don't let it touch your lips. Remember: every transmission reflects on your professionalism, so get this right.
exam_tip: Boom 2–3 cm from lips; key PTT, pause 0.25s, speak clearly; release when done.
key_facts: Headset snug but comfortable; boom angled slightly downward, positioned 2–3 cm from lips
PTT: press firmly, pause ~0.25 seconds before speaking to allow transmitter activation
Speak clearly and naturally; enunciate consonants distinctly, especially on HF
Release PTT after speaking; don't key intermittently during a single transmission
Monitor boom position in loud cockpits; pulling closer improves clarity without touching lips
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Describe the correct operation of: The handheld microphone
concept: If you're using a hand-held mic, there are a few things to remember. Hold it vertically about 5 centimeters from your mouth—not pressed against your lips, and not way out at arm's length. The microphone has a PTT button on the side or at the top; you'll press it with your thumb while you hold the mic in your hand. Same deal as the boom: press the button, pause a quarter-second, then speak. Your other hand's busy flying, so you need to be smooth and efficient. Hand-held mics are common in older aircraft and some trainers. The advantage is you've got full control and can move it around if you need to. The disadvantage is you're holding something while flying, which takes up a hand. When you key the mic, grip it firmly but not so tight your knuckles turn white. Speak into it at a slight angle if you're in a noisy cockpit. Test it the same way—ask for a radio check to make sure both Rx and Tx are working. If the cable gets kinked or the connector comes loose, you lose comms, so check that before flight.
exam_tip: Hold hand-held mic 5 cm from mouth; press PTT, pause, speak; ensure secure connection.
key_facts: Hand-held mic held vertically, ~5 cm from mouth; not against lips, not at arm's length
PTT button on side or top of mic; press firmly with thumb, pause ~0.25s before speaking
One hand occupied holding mic; requires smooth, efficient technique while flying
Cable and connector must be secure; loose connections cause Rx/Tx failure
Test hand-held mic operation via radio check; confirm both transmission and reception
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Describe the manipulation and adjustment of the controls of commonly used transponders.
concept: Your transponder's a pretty simple box once you understand what each knob does. There's usually a mode selector that lets you choose Standby (off), Mode A (altitude-less code only), Mode C (code plus altitude), or ALT (automatic). In NZ, you'll generally fly Mode C when ATC requires it—that sends your radar code and your altitude to their screens. The code selector knobs—usually four of them—let you dial in the four-digit squawk code ATC assigns you. They click into place, so you know when you've set the right number. Some transponders have a rotary knob instead of four separate knobs, but the principle's the same. There's usually a brightness or test button for checking it's working. And most modern ones have an Ident button—you press that to send a special pulse that highlights your target on ATC's radar. It lasts about 15 seconds, so don't hold it down. The key thing: know your aircraft's specific transponder before flight. Every model's slightly different, and you don't want to be learning controls during a check ride. Practice setting different codes on the ground until it's second nature.
exam_tip: Mode A/C selector; four-digit code knobs; Ident button highlights radar blip for ~15 secs.
key_facts: Mode selector: Standby (off), Mode A (code only), Mode C (code + altitude), ALT (automatic)
Code selection: four-digit squawk set via rotary knobs or individual digit selectors
Mode C transmission: sends 4-digit code and pressure altitude to ATC radar displays
Ident button: sends special pulse highlighting your target on radar for ~15 seconds
Test/brightness controls verify transponder operation; know your aircraft's specific transponder layout
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Describe the function of the following terms, and explain the procedures to transmit: Mode A information
concept: Mode A is your four-digit radar code—your identity on the radar screen. ATC assigns you a specific code for your flight, usually something like 2317 or 5421. You dial it in on the transponder before flight. This code stays with you for your whole flight in a given piece of airspace or with a particular controller. When you change hands between different ATCs—say, you're going from Christchurch to Wellington—you might get a new code. ATC tells you which code to select, and you read it back to confirm. You set it on the ground before takeoff, but you can change it in flight if ATC assigns a new one. Mode A tells the controller your aircraft's identity but nothing about your altitude. The radar shows your position, your altitude appears separately if you're Mode C, but your code's what identifies you. It's critical you're squawking the right code—if you're squawking someone else's code by accident, the controller can't tell which blip is which, and it's a safety issue. Always confirm the code verbally with ATC before you change it.
exam_tip: Mode A = 4-digit ID code; set as assigned; confirm verbally before changing.
key_facts: Mode A: 4-digit code identifying aircraft on radar; assigned by ATC for specific airspace/flight
Code range: 0000–7777 octal notation (NZ typically uses 1000–7700 range)
Pilot sets assigned code on transponder; confirms with ATC before entry or change
Code remains unchanged unless ATC assigns different code (change of ATC jurisdiction)
Incorrect code creates identification error; always verify code with ATC before transmission
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Describe the function of the following terms, and explain the procedures to transmit: Mode C information
concept: Mode C is Mode A plus altitude. Your transponder sends not just your code, but also your current altitude derived from your static pressure. ATC's radar now sees your altitude directly, which helps them track you and ensure separation. To use Mode C, you need an encoding altimeter—an altimeter that outputs your altitude to the transponder. Most modern aircraft have this. You select Mode C (or ALT, which is automatic Mode C) on your transponder, and away you go. The altitude data updates continuously as you climb or descend. ATC sees this in real time and can quickly spot if you're deviating from your assigned altitude. Mode C is mandatory in controlled airspace in NZ under CAR Part 91.249. If your transponder fails or you lose your encoding altimeter, you drop back to Mode A only and you need to notify ATC. That's why it's important to check your transponder's working Mode C during your preflight checks. Press the test button to see if it's responding. If Mode C fails but Mode A works, you can still fly, but ATC has less situational awareness, and you might need to request different routing.
exam_tip: Mode C = code + altitude from encoding altimeter; mandatory in NZ controlled airspace.
key_facts: Mode C: 4-digit code + altitude transmitted via encoding altimeter (static pressure input)
Altitude updates continuously; provides real-time altitude indication to ATC radar
Encoding altimeter required; outputs digital altitude signal to transponder
Mode C mandatory in NZ controlled airspace under CAR Part 91.249(a)
Mode C failure reduces ATC situational awareness; notify ATC and request instructions
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Unlawful interference code 7500
concept: Code 7500 is reserved for unlawful interference—hijacking, or any threat to the aircraft's safety by someone on board or attempting to force the aircraft to deviate. It's a serious situation. You'd only squawk 7500 if you genuinely believed the aircraft was being hijacked or threatened. Once you select 7500, ATC knows immediately that there's a major security issue. They'll treat it as an emergency, alert law enforcement, and coordinate your landing at a secure location. You don't need to talk about what's happening on the radio if it's ongoing. Just select 7500, and ATC will handle coordination. If you can communicate safely without revealing the threat, you might give them a brief indication, but your first action is to change the code. This is one of those situations where the transponder code itself communicates the emergency without words. The other two emergency codes you need to know are 7700 (general emergency/distress) and 7600 (radio failure). They're all important, but 7500 is the one you really hope you never have to use.
exam_tip: Code 7500 = unlawful interference; alerts ATC to hijacking or on-board security threat.
key_facts: Squawk 7500: unlawful interference (hijacking, threats to aircraft safety)
Selection triggers immediate ATC emergency response and law enforcement coordination
Pilot may remain silent if threat is ongoing; transponder code communicates emergency
Secure landing at appropriate location arranged by ATC and security authorities
Distinct from 7700 (general emergency) and 7600 (radio failure); each signals different emergency type
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: State the emergency codes and explain when they should be used.
concept: There are three transponder emergency codes you absolutely need to know. Code 7700 is for a general emergency or distress—engine failure, structural damage, medical emergency, anything that's gone seriously wrong and you need immediate assistance. Select 7700 and call the station (Christchurch, Auckland tower, whoever you're in contact with) and tell them 'Mayday' what's happening. Code 7600 is radio failure. Your radio's dead and you can't talk to anyone. You select 7600 so ATC can see on their radar that you've lost comms. You can't tell them what happened over the radio obviously, so the code itself is your message. ATC will track you and probably use light signals to give you instructions—steady green means you're cleared to land, red means hold off. Code 7500, as we covered, is unlawful interference. The golden rule: once you squawk an emergency code, you're signaling a serious situation. ATC will respond immediately and treat you as top priority. You'll typically want to declare the emergency verbally too—'Mayday, Mayday' for 7700, or if you have partial comms, explain the situation. Get to a safe place and land at the nearest suitable aerodrome.
exam_tip: 7700 = distress/emergency; 7600 = radio failure; 7500 = unlawful interference.
key_facts: Code 7700: general emergency/distress; select and declare 'Mayday' with nature of emergency
Code 7600: radio failure; select code as communication when unable to transmit voice
Code 7500: unlawful interference/hijacking; silent selection alerts ATC to threat
Emergency squawk triggers top-priority ATC response and emergency services coordination
Land at nearest suitable aerodrome; provide situation reports when able to communicate
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Demonstrate proficiency in transponder terminology and describe the actions and responses expected from a pilot, following ATC transponder instructions.
concept: When ATC gives you a transponder instruction, they'll say something like 'Zulu Kilo Alpha Bravo Charlie, squawk 3417, Mode Charlie' or just 'squawk 3417'. You read back the code to confirm: 'Squawk 3417, Zulu Kilo Alpha Bravo Charlie'. Then you set it on your transponder. ATC expects you to acknowledge when you've set the code and to confirm it's selected. If you select it wrong and ATC doesn't catch it immediately, they might see your blip and notice it doesn't match your callsign on their display. They'll call you up and ask for a re-check. 'Zulu Kilo Alpha Bravo Charlie, verify code 2417'—that means check what code you've got selected and tell them. You'll look at your transponder and confirm it's set correctly, or you'll correct it if it's wrong. If ATC asks you to 'ident', you press the Ident button briefly—just one quick tap—and you'll say 'Ident', so they know you've done it. They'll see your target flash on their radar. Always read back any transponder instruction, always confirm you've selected it, and if ATC questions a code, never argue—just check your transponder and correct it. They've got more information than you do.
exam_tip: Readback: repeat code and confirm selection; respond to 'verify' by checking your actual setting.
key_facts: ATC instruction format: 'Squawk [code]' or 'Squawk [code], Mode [A/C]'
Pilot readback: '[Code], [Callsign]' to confirm understanding and intent
Pilot confirms actual selection: acknowledge once set, confirm again if requested
'Ident' request: brief press of Ident button; pilot acknowledges 'Ident' on radio
'Verify' request: check transponder and report actual code set; correct if in error
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: List the documents that identify transponder mandatory airspace.
concept: Transponder mandatory airspace in NZ is defined in a few key documents. The most important is your NZ Civil Aviation Rules Part 91.249, which outlines exactly where you need a transponder and what mode it needs to be in. You've also got the Airways NZ flight information publications, specifically the Aeronautical Information Publication (AIP), which lists the transponder mandatory areas and their specific rules. The AIP includes charts showing which airspace requires Mode C, which requires just Mode A, and where transponders are optional. When you're planning a flight, you check the AIP for your route. If you're flying through controlled airspace or busy terminal areas, you can bet you'll need a transponder. Auckland, Christchurch, and Wellington all have transponder mandatory zones. If your transponder's unserviceable, you need to notify ATC before flight and request a routing that avoids transponder mandatory airspace—they might give you an alternative route, or they might not allow the flight until it's fixed. The AIP's your go-to document, but check with the specific ATC unit if you've got any doubt. They're the experts on what's required for your specific flight.
exam_tip: CAR Part 91.249 and NZ AIP define transponder mandatory airspace and mode requirements.
key_facts: CAR Part 91.249(a): transponder mandatory airspace identified in NZ Civil Aviation Rules
NZ AIP (Aeronautical Information Publication): detailed charts and descriptions of TMAs
Airways NZ ATC circulars and NOTAMs may update or clarify transponder requirements
Controlled airspace (Class A, B, C) typically requires Mode C in NZ
Unserviceable transponder: notify ATC; request amended routing or defer flight
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Describe the procedures for operations in transponder mandatory airspace when the aircraft transponder is inoperative.
concept: If your transponder goes unserviceable and you're supposed to be in transponder mandatory airspace—say, the Auckland TMA—you can't just fly through. Here's what you do. First, before you take off, you contact ATC and declare the transponder unserviceable. You'll say something like 'Auckland Control, Zulu Kilo Alpha Bravo Charlie, request routing with unserviceable transponder'. ATC might offer you an alternative route that avoids the mandatory area, or they might clear you through but with restrictions—like a specific altitude or heading to keep you out of conflicting traffic. If ATC can't accommodate you, they'll tell you no. In that case, you don't fly through transponder mandatory airspace. If your transponder fails during flight—say, you're already in the airspace—you declare the failure immediately: 'Auckland Control, Zulu Kilo Alpha Bravo Charlie, transponder mode C unserviceable'. ATC will give you instructions. They might ask you to change course, climb or descend, or sequence you with other traffic. They can still track you on radar even without the transponder, but they've lost your altitude information if you were Mode C, so they'll ask for altitude reports. Listen carefully to their instructions and follow them exactly.
exam_tip: Unserviceable transponder: notify ATC before flight; request amended routing or accept restrictions.
key_facts: Pre-flight declaration: contact ATC, state transponder unserviceable, request routing
ATC response: offer alternative route, clear with restrictions, or deny entry to TMA
In-flight failure: declare immediately; expect altitude reports and vectors from ATC
ATC can track radar blip but loses altitude data; pilot must provide altitude reports
Comply with all ATC instructions; may be vectors, altitude changes, or speed restrictions
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: State the frequency(ies) on which the ELT transmits.
concept: Your ELT transmits on two frequencies simultaneously: 406 MHz and 121.5 MHz. The 406 MHz frequency is the satellite frequency. If your aircraft's down in a remote area, a 406 MHz signal can be picked up by Cospas-Sarsat satellites and relayed to rescue authorities. That's your lifeline if you crash in the Southern Alps or out the back of beyond. The 121.5 MHz is the homing frequency. Once rescuers have a general location from the satellite detection, they can home in on the 121.5 MHz signal with a portable direction-finding receiver. So together, these two frequencies give you a two-step rescue: first, detection by satellite if you're remote; second, pinpoint location by searchers using hand-held equipment. The 406 MHz frequency is the big one for NZ because so much of our country is remote and inaccessible—it's the difference between being found in two days or two weeks. 121.5 MHz was the original ELT frequency, and it's still used, but it's not monitored as reliably as it used to be. That's why the 406 MHz satellite system is so important nowadays.
exam_tip: ELT transmits 406 MHz (satellite detection) and 121.5 MHz (homing).
key_facts: 406 MHz: satellite frequency detected by Cospas-Sarsat satellites; relayed to rescue authorities
121.5 MHz: homing frequency for searchers with direction-finding receivers
Dual frequency provides detection (satellite) + homing (portable DF) capability
406 MHz signal crucial in remote NZ terrain where satellite detection provides first alert
ELT registration via 406 MHz allows RCC NZ to identify aircraft and POB via satellite
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for the carriage of an ELT.
concept: Under NZ Civil Aviation Rules Part 91.509, if you're flying an aircraft over water or in remote terrain—which, let's face it, includes most of NZ—you need to carry an ELT. Most aircraft have one installed. The ELT needs to be current and registered. The registration's important because when the satellite picks up your 406 MHz signal, it includes your aircraft's registration data, so rescuers immediately know who they're looking for, where you were going, and who else might have been with you. If you move the ELT from one aircraft to another, you need to update the registration. The ELT also needs to be serviced and tested regularly—your maintenance crew looks after that, but you need to check during your preflight that it looks intact and hasn't been damaged. You'll see a little placard near the ELT with the activation date and next service due. If it's overdue for service, you need to get it sorted before flying. An ELT that's not registered or overdue for maintenance is almost useless if you actually need it. You're flying over remote terrain from Ardmore to the West Coast—you absolutely need a serviceable, registered ELT.
exam_tip: CAR Part 91.509: ELT required for operations over water/remote terrain; must be registered and serviced.
key_facts: CAR Part 91.509: ELT carriage required for aircraft operating over water or remote terrain
ELT must be registered; registration data transmitted with 406 MHz signal to rescuers
Service and maintenance: ELT requires periodic servicing; check placards for next service date
Preflight: verify ELT installed, intact, and within service interval
Update registration if ELT moved to different aircraft; inform AIS of any changes
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Explain how an ELT can be activated: Automatically in the event of an impact
concept: Most ELTs are equipped with what's called a 'g-switch'—it's a little mechanical device inside the ELT that detects sudden deceleration from a crash impact. If you hit the ground hard enough to trigger that switch (generally around 5g of impact), the ELT automatically powers up and starts transmitting on both 406 and 121.5 MHz. You don't have to do anything. The activation is automatic, which is brilliant because if you're seriously injured or unconscious after the crash, help's already on the way. The g-switch is pretty sensitive—it won't go off from normal landing turbulence or a hard bump, but an actual crash will do it. If your ELT has been in an accident, the g-switch might already be activated even if it looks fine. That's why after a hard landing, you need to check whether your ELT's transmitting. Listen on 121.5 MHz—if you hear the ELT's distinctive warbling tone, you know it's active. If it goes off accidentally, you've got options: you can stop it manually (we'll cover that next), or you need to notify ATC immediately so they don't launch a full-scale rescue operation.
exam_tip: G-switch in ELT activates automatically on impact (~5g deceleration).
key_facts: G-switch: mechanical deceleration sensor in ELT; triggers auto-activation on impact (~5g)
Auto-activation requires no pilot action; transmission begins immediately on impact
Check ELT status after hard landing; listen on 121.5 MHz for warble tone
Hard landing may trigger g-switch even if aircraft appears undamaged; verify ELT status
If accidentally activated, manually deactivate or declare to ATC to avoid false SAR response
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Explain how an ELT can be activated: Manually
concept: You can also activate your ELT manually. There's usually a switch or lever somewhere in the cockpit or cargo area—your POH tells you where. It might be a toggle switch, a rotary switch with an 'ON' position, or a pull handle. If you've had an accident and your ELT's g-switch didn't activate, or if the g-switch is intermittent and you think there's a problem, you can flip that switch to ON to manually activate transmission. You'd do this after a crash landing, or if you've had to make a forced landing in a remote area and you want rescuers to know you're down. Once you manually activate it, it'll keep transmitting until you switch it off or the battery dies. That's why you only do it when there's a genuine emergency. If you accidentally activate it on the ground—say, during a maintenance check—you need to switch it off immediately and let ATC know it was accidental. A false activation triggers a costly SAR response, but they'd rather you call and admit the mistake than let them launch helicopters for no reason. So know where your ELT activation switch is, know how it works, but don't touch it unless you've genuinely got an emergency.
exam_tip: Manual activation: switch/lever in cockpit; use only in genuine emergency to avoid false SAR.
key_facts: ELT manual activation: switch or lever in cockpit/cargo area; check POH for exact location
Manual activation used if g-switch failed or for forced landing in remote area
Transmits continuously until manually switched OFF or battery depleted
Accidental activation: switch OFF immediately and notify ATC of false alarm
Coordinate with ATC regarding status; false activation triggers SAR response
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Describe the management of the ELT following a forced landing.
concept: So you've made a forced landing—engine failure, fuel mismanagement, whatever it was. You're down safely but you're in a remote spot. Your first priorities are crew and passenger safety, then stabilizing the aircraft. Then you deal with the ELT. If the g-switch activated it automatically, it's already transmitting and help's on the way. If it didn't, you'll manually activate it. Once the ELT's transmitting, rescue authorities have your location and are coordinating. Now, here's the key: you stay with the aircraft. Aircraft are much easier to spot from the air than people walking around in the bush, and your ELT's signal's strongest inside or right next to the aircraft. Check the ELT's transmitting by listening on 121.5 MHz—you should hear the distinctive warble. Set up your emergency shelter, make the aircraft visible (open doors, use bright fabric, etc.), and try to conserve battery power if you can. Don't wander off. If you've got a working radio, you can talk to rescuers and give them updates. If your radio's also out, just stay put. Under CAR Part 91.515, after a forced landing, you need to ensure the ELT keeps transmitting—don't switch it off unless rescuers specifically tell you to. The 406 MHz signal's already been detected by satellite, so even if you lose the 121.5 MHz homing signal, rescuers are on the way.
exam_tip: Post forced landing: ELT transmitting, stay with aircraft, make it visible, conserve batteries.
key_facts: Forced landing: establish crew safety, stabilize aircraft, then activate/verify ELT
Check ELT transmission by listening on 121.5 MHz for warble tone
Stay with aircraft; it's easier to spot than people; ELT signal strongest near aircraft
Make aircraft visible: open doors, deploy bright fabric, establish ground-to-air signals
CAR Part 91.515: maintain ELT transmission until directed otherwise by rescue authorities
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Describe the requirements associated with ELT testing.
concept: ELT testing's strictly controlled because false activations trigger expensive search and rescue operations. Your ELT can only be tested during a specific time window—the first five minutes of each hour, on any day. So between 0900:00 and 0905:00, or 1010:00 and 1010:59, those are your testing windows. When you test, you're only doing a brief transmission. On 121.5 MHz, you might transmit for a few seconds to confirm the ELT's working, but you're not supposed to do more than three brief sweeps in that five-minute window. You want to avoid triggering a full SAR response. If you're in the air testing, you need to notify ATC first so they know it's a test and don't pass it on to rescue. Make sure they understand it's just a test—say 'Auckland Control, Zulu Kilo Alpha Bravo Charlie, ELT test' or similar. If you're testing on the ground and the ELT activates, listen for that warble on 121.5 MHz, then switch it off and verify it's stopped before you leave the aircraft. Your maintenance crew'll do more thorough testing during servicing, but day-to-day checks by pilots are quick and limited to that five-minute window. If you accidentally go outside that window, immediately switch off the ELT and declare it to ATC.
exam_tip: ELT test only: first 5 min of each hour; max 3 brief sweeps; notify ATC if airborne.
key_facts: ELT testing window: first 5 minutes of each hour (0900:00–0900:59, 1000:00–1000:59, etc.)
Airborne test: notify ATC explicitly 'ELT test'; brief transmission only
Ground test: verify warble on 121.5 MHz, then switch OFF; max 3 brief sweeps
Outside test window: switch ELT OFF immediately; declare false activation to ATC
Maintenance: comprehensive testing performed during scheduled service; check service date placards
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Explain the procedures to follow in the case of inadvertent ELT activation.
concept: It happens. You've been doing maintenance, or a hard landing jostled things, and your ELT's suddenly transmitting. First thing: don't panic. You've got time. Second: switch it OFF immediately. Find that activation switch in your POH and toggle it back to standby or OFF. Listen on 121.5 MHz to confirm the warble's gone. Once it's off, you need to get on the radio and tell ATC immediately: 'Auckland Control, Zulu Kilo Alpha Bravo Charlie, ELT inadvertent activation, ELT now off'. Be clear and concise. Don't bury the lede. The sooner ATC knows it was accidental, the sooner they can cancel any SAR response that might've been initiated. Rescue authorities respond to ELT activations pretty quickly, so speed matters here. If your ELT's activated on the ground while you're doing maintenance, and you're not even in the aircraft, you still need to declare it and confirm you've switched it off. Your maintenance crew should have checked it was safe, but mistakes happen. Tell the tower what happened and why it's now off. If it was during the test window, you're fine—just confirm it was a test. If it was outside the test window and completely accidental, explain that. ATC and rescue will appreciate the heads-up and can stand down instead of scrambling.
exam_tip: Inadvertent activation: switch OFF immediately; notify ATC; confirm off on 121.5 MHz.
key_facts: Inadvertent activation: switch ELT OFF first; listen on 121.5 MHz to confirm silence
Notify ATC immediately: state ELT inadvertent activation, confirm OFF
Provide explanation (e.g., hard landing, maintenance check) to assist ATC response
SAR may already be alerted; early notification allows cancellation before dispatch
Avoid repeated transmissions; single brief OFF declaration sufficient for ATC awareness
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Demonstrate proficiency in transmitting and receiving spoken messages competently and in accordance with prescribed procedures, including: Language to be used
concept: English is the international aviation language. You use English on all aviation frequencies in NZ, no exceptions. But it's not conversational English—it's standardized, professional English with specific terminology. You speak clearly and at a moderate pace. Avoid slang, colloquialisms, or local dialect. ATC in Christchurch needs to understand you just as clearly as a pilot calling from Auckland. Avoid contractions in formal calls, though you'll hear controllers and some pilots use them casually. Don't try to be clever or funny. 'We're a bit lost, can you help?' is not the way to ask for navigation assistance. Instead: 'Request radar vectors to Ardmore'. Keep transmissions brief and to the point. Every word should carry information. If you're trying to sound professional and you're not comfortable, just speak clearly and naturally—that beats trying to imitate some Hollywood pilot. One thing: if you're not sure of a term or procedure, ask. Controllers appreciate a pilot who asks 'Say again' or 'Standby' rather than one who pretends to understand and makes a mistake. Your professionalism isn't about fancy language—it's about clear, accurate communication.
exam_tip: Use standardized English; clear, moderate pace; no slang/contractions; professional terminology.
key_facts: English: official aviation language in NZ; all frequencies require English transmissions
Speak clearly at moderate pace; avoid slang, contractions, local dialect, colloquialisms
Terminology: standardized phraseology reduces ambiguity and improves clarity
Brevity: transmit only necessary information; remove filler words and unnecessary detail
Professionalism: accurate terminology and clear speech reflect competence; ask if unsure
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Demonstrate proficiency in transmitting and receiving spoken messages competently and in accordance with prescribed procedures, including: Word spelling
concept: When you need to spell something on the radio—a fix name, a surname, an aircraft type—you use the ICAO phonetic alphabet. Alpha, Bravo, Charlie, Delta, Echo, Foxtrot, Golf, Hotel, India, Juliet, Kilo, Lima, Mike, November, Oscar, Papa, Quebec, Romeo, Sierra, Tango, Uniform, Victor, Whiskey, X-ray, Yankee, Zulu. That's international standard, and everyone in aviation knows it. If you're spelling out something like 'Milford Sound' to ATC, you'd say 'Mike India Lima Foxtrot Oscar Romeo Delta'. Practice these until they're automatic—you don't want to be sitting in the cockpit going 'M like... um... Mike?' You'll also use this for aircraft registration marks. ZK-ABC becomes 'Zulu Kilo Alpha Bravo Charlie'. Some phonetics might seem odd—X-ray instead of 'X', Juliet instead of 'June'—but they're chosen for clarity on bad radio reception. Nobody's going to mistake Charlie for any other letter. Get them right, use them consistently, and you'll be understood every time.
exam_tip: Spell using ICAO phonetic alphabet (Alpha, Bravo, Charlie, etc.); learn and practice thoroughly.
key_facts: ICAO phonetic alphabet: 26 words (Alpha–Zulu) standardized internationally
Used for spelling: callsigns (ZK-ABC = Zulu Kilo Alpha Bravo Charlie), location names, surnames
Each word chosen for clarity on degraded radio reception; practice until automatic
Consistency: same phonetic for same letter every time; no improvisation or variation
Apply to aircraft registration, pilot names, fix names, any spelling necessary for clarity
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Demonstrate proficiency in transmitting and receiving spoken messages competently and in accordance with prescribed procedures, including: Transmission of numerals
concept: Numbers are transmitted digit by digit, with one exception: whole hundreds and whole thousands are said as units. So 2000 feet is 'two thousand feet', and 1500 is 'one five zero zero feet'. But if it's not a round number like 2300 feet, you say it: 'two three zero zero feet'. Runway numbers are said as two digits: Runway 04 is 'zero four', Runway 18 is 'one eight'. Frequency 121.5 is 'one two one decimal five'. Altimeter setting 1013 is 'one zero one three'. Squawk codes are all four digits: 3417 is 'three four one seven'. On HF, which can be noisy, you might hear people saying numbers slowly and emphasizing them, because it's hard to catch. If you're not sure what number you heard, ask: 'Say again'. Don't guess. In the exam, you might be asked 'what's 2450 feet?' and the answer is 'two four five zero' because 2450 is not a round number. Get used to saying 'say again' or 'standby' if you didn't catch a number—it's much better to ask than to assume.
exam_tip: Digits separately, except whole hundreds/thousands (e.g., 2000='two thousand', 2400='two four zero zero').
key_facts: Default: each digit pronounced separately (2417 = 'two four one seven')
Whole hundreds: 'one hundred', 'two hundred', etc. (100 = 'one hundred')
Whole thousands: 'one thousand', 'two thousand', etc. (2000 = 'two thousand')
Runway numbers: two digits (04 = 'zero four', 18 = 'one eight')
Frequencies and altimeter settings: digit by digit (121.5 = 'one two one decimal five')
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Demonstrate proficiency in transmitting and receiving spoken messages competently and in accordance with prescribed procedures, including: Procedure words and phrases
concept: Aviation always uses UTC—Zulu time—for all communications and flight planning. No exceptions. Your altimeter, your flight plan, your radio calls, everything's in UTC. When ATC gives you time, it's UTC. When you report time to ATC, it's UTC. You'll often see it written as just numbers: 1430Z means 14 hours 30 minutes Zulu, which is 2:30 PM. On the radio, you might say 'at two seven five zero Zulu' for 2750Z. Time's given as four digits: hours and minutes only, no seconds. So 0930 Zulu is 09 hours 30 minutes. In NZ, we're UTC+12 in winter, UTC+13 in summer, so there's an offset between our wall clock and Zulu. Your watch shows NZ time, but you translate to UTC for all aviation purposes. If it's 1000 NZST (winter), that's 2200 UTC the previous day. This trips people up, so practice converting. When ATC says 'see you at zero six zero zero Zulu', you need to know whether that's tomorrow or today based on your local time. It's a critical skill, not just for exams but for flight planning and safety.
exam_tip: Aviation time = UTC (Zulu); 4 digits (HHMM); convert local NZ time to UTC.
key_facts: Aviation communications: UTC (Zulu) only; no local time references
Time format: 4 digits (hours + minutes), e.g., 1430 Zulu = 14 hours 30 minutes
NZ offsets: UTC+12 (NZST, winter), UTC+13 (NZDT, summer)
Flight planning: all reports, ETAs, and times in UTC for clarity across time zones
Conversion practice: know local-to-UTC offset for accurate timing in flight operations
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Demonstrate proficiency in transmitting and receiving spoken messages competently and in accordance with prescribed procedures, including: Time system
concept: Aviation has standardized phraseology for a reason: everyone knows what everyone else means. 'Affirm' means yes, 'negative' means no. Don't use 'yeah' or 'no'—it's unprofessional and on a bad radio, 'yeah' can sound like a negative. 'Roger' means 'I have received and understood your last transmission'; it doesn't mean 'yes' or 'I agree'. 'Wilco' means 'I will comply'. 'Say again' means repeat what you said; 'say again all after' means repeat from a particular point. 'Standby' means wait, I'm busy. 'Unable' means you can't do what was requested. 'Request' is how you ask for something. 'Cleared' is ATC giving you permission. There are dozens of these standard phrases in the AC 91-9 manual. Learn them. They're not fancy—they're designed for clarity and brevity. If you use them consistently, controllers trust that you understand procedures and can follow instructions safely. If you're making up your own words, it raises questions about your training and competence. So spend time learning the standard phraseology. It's not hard—it's actually easier than trying to sound natural because the patterns repeat constantly.
exam_tip: Memorize standard phraseology: 'affirm', 'negative', 'roger', 'wilco', 'standby', 'request', 'cleared'.
key_facts: Affirm: yes; Negative: no (never 'yeah' or 'no' on radio)
Roger: received and understood (not agreement or affirmation)
Wilco: will comply with instruction; implies understanding and commitment
Say again: repeat entire previous transmission; Say again [phrase]: repeat from that point
Cleared: ATC authorization to proceed; Request: pilot asking for something
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Demonstrate proficiency in transmitting and receiving spoken messages competently and in accordance with prescribed procedures, including: Establishment of communications
concept: Your aircraft's callsign is its registration mark with the ICAO phonetic alphabet. ZK-ABC is 'Zulu Kilo Alpha Bravo Charlie'. That's your full callsign. You use it for initial contact with any frequency. Once you've established contact and ATC knows who you are, you can abbreviate. ATC might say 'Zulu Kilo Alpha Bravo Charlie, abbreviated callsign Zulu Kilo Bravo Charlie' or just 'abbreviated to Alpha Bravo Charlie'. From then on, you use that short form. 'Alpha Bravo Charlie, descend to 3000 feet'. Makes sense—saves time. But if you lose contact for any reason or change frequencies, you go back to your full callsign for the initial contact. Your callsign's on your certificate, on your registration documentation, on the aircraft itself. You need to know it cold. If you're flying a Cessna 172 with registration ZK-DEF, every radio call starts with that. And when you spell it, you use the phonetic: Delta Echo Foxtrot. Don't abbreviate on initial contact, don't make up call signs, and don't use the aircraft type as a callsign unless you're told to. That might be okay for some operations, but not for PPL.
exam_tip: Full callsign = registration (ZK-ABC = Zulu Kilo Alpha Bravo Charlie); abbreviate only after ATC establishes.
key_facts: Full callsign: aircraft registration spelled in ICAO phonetic (ZK-ABC = Zulu Kilo Alpha Bravo Charlie)
Initial contact: always use full callsign on new frequency or after loss of contact
Abbreviated callsign: used only after ATC establishes and authorizes abbreviation
Abbreviation examples: 'Alpha Bravo Charlie' or 'Zulu Kilo Bravo Charlie' per ATC instruction
Aircraft type not used as callsign for PPL; use registration only
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Demonstrate proficiency in transmitting and receiving spoken messages competently and in accordance with prescribed procedures, including: Frequencies to be used
concept: Establishing contact means getting someone's attention on the radio so they know you're there and listening. Your first transmission should be brief: 'Christchurch Information, Zulu Kilo Alpha Bravo Charlie, request QNH'. That tells them who you are, what frequency they're on (implicit by you calling them), and what you want. They'll respond with the information or ask you to standby. Once they've acknowledged you, you maintain contact by acknowledging their transmissions: 'Christchurch, Zulu Kilo Bravo Charlie' (the abbreviated callsign now). Continuation means keeping the frequency active between you and them. If they give you an instruction, you read it back so they know you understood. If there's a long silence, don't fill it with chatter—silence is fine. But if you need something, you make your request clear. If they say 'Standby', you wait and keep listening. If they say 'Frequency change to 119.5', you change and call them on the new frequency with your initial contact call again. The key: establish who you are, be clear about what you want, acknowledge everything important, and don't transmit unless you've got something to say. Continuation is mostly about disciplined listening and prompt compliance.
exam_tip: Initial contact: ID + station + request; continuation: acknowledge, readback critical info, maintain listening watch.
key_facts: Initial contact: '[Station], [Full Callsign], [Request]' e.g., 'Christchurch Information, Zulu Kilo Alpha Bravo Charlie, request QNH'
Acknowledgment: brief response confirming receipt (e.g., 'Zulu Kilo Bravo Charlie')
Readback: repeat back critical information (frequencies, codes, instructions) to confirm understanding
Frequency change: on new frequency, reestablish with full callsign
Listening watch: maintain frequency awareness; don't transmit unless necessary
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Demonstrate proficiency in transmitting and receiving spoken messages competently and in accordance with prescribed procedures, including: Identification of service
concept: When you're calling a ground service, you need to know what it's called and which frequency it's on. In NZ, we have several types of services. Christchurch Information, Wellington Information, Auckland Control, Hamilton Radio—these are all different services with different functions, and they're on different frequencies. Christchurch Information on 119.1 MHz gives flight information service—weather, QNH, airspace info. Auckland Control manages controlled airspace. Milford Sound has its own information frequency. Before you call, check your AIP or your flight planning documentation and know which service you need and what frequency they're on. When you call, you identify them clearly: 'Christchurch Information, Zulu Kilo Alpha Bravo Charlie, at Ardmore, request current QNH'. That tells them where you are and what you want. If you call the wrong frequency, you'll either get no response or someone will politely tell you you're on the wrong frequency. No big deal—just move to the right one. But doing your homework beforehand saves time and embarrassment. Know your frequencies, know what each service does, and identify them correctly on the radio.
exam_tip: Identify service by name (e.g., Christchurch Information, Auckland Control); use correct frequency.
key_facts: NZ ground services: Information (FIS), Control (ATC), Radio, AFIS, UNICOM on designated frequencies
Christchurch Information: 119.1 MHz (main FIS for much of NZ)
Service type determines function: FIS provides information; Control provides clearances
Call identification: '[Service Name], [Callsign], [Location], [Request]'
Verify frequency and service function via AIP before transmission
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Demonstrate proficiency in transmitting and receiving spoken messages competently and in accordance with prescribed procedures, including: Radiotelephony aircraft callsigns
concept: Communication discipline means knowing when to talk and when not to. It's about respect for the frequency and for the people trying to work. Don't transmit unless you've got something important to say. Don't fill silence with chatter. Don't interrupt another aircraft's transmission. If you hear someone else on frequency, wait until they're done before you key the mike. If the frequency's busy, you listen first and wait for a pause. 'Communication discipline' includes not getting emotional on the radio. If something goes wrong or ATC doesn't give you what you wanted, you remain professional. You don't argue, you don't get frustrated. You can disagree—'request different routing due to turbulence' is fine—but you do it calmly. If you make a mistake, acknowledge it, correct it, and move on. That's professional. Discipline also means not using the radio for socializing once you've moved to a different frequency. Once-off transmissions are fine—'Zulu Kilo Bravo Charlie is a Cessna 172'—but lengthy conversations belong on the ground or on the phone. Keep transmissions brief, concise, and purposeful. This is what separates pilots who sound experienced from those who don't.
exam_tip: Transmit only when necessary; wait for pauses; remain calm and professional; brief, concise calls.
key_facts: Listen first: hear frequency status before transmitting; don't interrupt others
Silence is acceptable: don't fill with unnecessary chatter or acknowledgments
Brief transmissions: concise, purposeful messages; remove filler words
Professional demeanor: remain calm even if frustrated; no arguments or emotion on radio
One purpose per call: avoid lengthy conversations; separate requests as needed
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Demonstrate proficiency in transmitting and receiving spoken messages competently and in accordance with prescribed procedures, including: Procedures for exchange of messages
concept: Microphone technique is about producing clear, understandable audio. You've already learned about positioning the boom mic 2–3 cm from your mouth. That's the hardware side. On the transmission side, speak naturally and clearly. Don't shout—the transmitter will distort and you'll sound worse. Speak at a normal volume and moderate pace. Emphasize consonants, especially on HF where the signal's degraded. 'Christchurch' should come through as clearly as 'Charlie Bravo'. Some people slow down slightly on HF, and that's fine—it gives the signal time to carry. But don't slow down so much that it sounds unnatural. Enunciate each word. If you've got a heavy accent or English is your second language, that's okay—just speak clearly and slowly enough that people can understand. On a bad radio, people can forgive accents but they can't forgive mumbling. Pause between phrases if you're giving complex information. 'Request vectors to Ardmore. Current altitude 2000 feet.' Not 'requestvectortoArdmorecurrentaltitude2000feet'. Let people's brains catch up. And remember: when you press the PTT, there's a quarter-second delay before the transmitter powers up. So pause briefly before speaking. That first word gets cut off if you don't.
exam_tip: Speak clearly at normal pace; emphasize consonants; pause before speaking after PTT press.
key_facts: Speak at natural, moderate pace; don't shout (causes distortion)
Emphasize consonants (especially on HF); clear enunciation is critical
Pause briefly after pressing PTT (~0.25s) before speaking; prevents first-word cutoff
Natural rhythm: pause between phrases to allow listener comprehension
Accent acceptable; clarity and enunciation are more important than perfect pronunciation
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Demonstrate proficiency in transmitting and receiving spoken messages competently and in accordance with prescribed procedures, including: Corrections and repetition tests
concept: Readback is one of the most important safety procedures in aviation. When ATC gives you critical information—especially control instructions—you read it back to confirm you've got it right. This catches misunderstandings before they become accidents. Critical information includes: runway in use, altimeter setting (QNH), transponder codes, level instructions (altitude), heading instructions, speed instructions, airways clearances, approach clearances, takeoff and landing clearances. If ATC says 'Zulu Kilo Bravo Charlie, descend to 2500 feet, QNH 1013', you read back: 'Descending to 2500 feet, QNH 1013, Zulu Kilo Bravo Charlie'. If there's something you didn't catch or that doesn't make sense, this is where you'd say 'Say again' or ask for clarification. For non-critical information—weather, procedural info, flight service information—you don't necessarily read back, but an acknowledgment is good. The readback's a double-check. ATC's listening to make sure you've understood correctly. If you read back '3500 feet' when they said '2500 feet', they'll catch it and correct you before it becomes a problem. Readback's not a sign of weakness or forgetfulness—it's professional discipline and a safety habit.
exam_tip: Readback critical info: runway, QNH, transponder code, altitude, heading, speed, clearances.
key_facts: Readback required: runway in use, QNH altimeter setting, transponder codes, level instructions
Readback required: heading instructions, speed instructions, clearances, approach/takeoff/landing clearances
Format: repeat back information + callsign (e.g., 'Runway 04, QNH 1013, Zulu Kilo Bravo Charlie')
ATC verifies readback; catches errors before they become operational problems
Disagreement with instruction: clarify via 'say again' or 'request different routing' before readback
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Demonstrate proficiency in transmitting and receiving spoken messages competently and in accordance with prescribed procedures, including: Listening out
concept: You report when ATC asks for a report, or when procedures require it at certain points. Position reports are the most common. At unattended aerodromes with a Mandatory Broadcast Zone, you transmit position reports so other aircraft know where you are. At a place like Ardmore, if you're approaching on the downwind leg for Runway 20, you might say 'All stations, Zulu Kilo Bravo Charlie, downwind Runway 20, Ardmore'. That tells everyone in the area what you're doing. In controlled airspace, ATC usually directs your reports: 'Report established on the approach'. You then report when you're established on the approach path. Reports should include your callsign, your position, your altitude or flight level, and your intended action if it's not clear. 'Christchurch Information, Zulu Kilo Bravo Charlie, thirty miles southwest of Christchurch, 2000 feet, inbound to Ardmore' tells them who you are, where you are, your altitude, and where you're going. Under CAR Part 91.247, you're required to report in certain circumstances. Check the rules and know when you need to report. Failure to report when required can get you a stern call from ATC and possibly enforcement action. So know the requirement for your airspace and altitude.
exam_tip: Position report = callsign + position + altitude + intention; provide when requested or required.
key_facts: Position report format: '[Station], [Callsign], [Position], [Altitude], [Intention]'
Mandatory Broadcast Zone (MBZ): prefix 'All stations' and aerodrome name; broadcast position reports
Controlled airspace: report as directed by ATC (e.g., 'Report established')
CAR Part 91.247: requires position reports in specific circumstances (check rules)
Omit unnecessary detail; include callsign, position, altitude, and next action
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Demonstrate proficiency in transmitting and receiving spoken messages competently and in accordance with prescribed procedures, including: Readability scale
concept: Test transmissions are how you check that your radio's working. You don't just turn it on and assume it'll work. A dead radio during flight's a real emergency. Before you start flying, when you're on the ground, you'll do a radio test. You might transmit on the aerodrome's frequency or a designated test frequency and say something like 'Ardmore, Zulu Kilo Bravo Charlie, radio check'. Someone will respond with your signal strength. Standard response is 'Zulu Kilo Bravo Charlie, you are strength five', meaning you're coming through loud and clear. If you get 'strength three', your transmission's weak but readable. If there's no response, something's wrong—check your headset connection, your frequency, your volume, your PTT. Most aerodromes have dedicated ground frequencies for radio checks. In remote areas with no tower, you might do a brief transmission on the CTAF frequency and hope someone's listening. The key is: test the radio before flight, not during. If it's dead on the ground, you can sort it out. If it's dead in the air, you're in trouble. During the check, you're looking for both directions of communication—you transmit and receive a response. That confirms both Tx and Rx are working.
exam_tip: Radio check before flight on ground frequency; request signal report; verify Tx and Rx working.
key_facts: Test transmission: '[Aerodrome], [Callsign], radio check' before flight
Signal strength response: strength 1 (unreadable) through strength 5 (loud and clear)
Verify both transmit and receive: test confirms two-way communication functioning
Troubleshoot on ground: volume, squelch, frequency, headset connections, spare radio
Confidence in radio operation: critical safety item; never fly with untested radio
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Demonstrate a good working knowledge of the following Civil Aviation Rules:
concept: NZ Civil Aviation Rules, Part 91, set the legal requirements for flight operations and radio use. As a pilot, you're bound by these rules, and the exam expects you to know the relevant ones. We're going to cover several specific sections, but the big picture is this: the rules exist to keep you, your passengers, and other aircraft safe. They cover everything from radio procedures to emergency management. You need to know these cold—not just for the exam, but because you'll be following them every time you fly. Each rule is numbered and has specific requirements. Some are brief, some are detailed. The exam might ask you to explain what a rule means in practical terms, or to apply it to a scenario. So as we go through each rule, think about why it exists. Why does the CAA require transponder operation in certain airspace? Because it keeps traffic separated. Why do we have specific radio phraseology? Because it reduces misunderstandings. Understanding the 'why' behind each rule will help you remember them and apply them correctly when you're in the cockpit.
exam_tip: CAR Part 91 governs operations, radio procedures, and safety; know specific sections 91.217–91.529.
key_facts: CAR Part 91: New Zealand Civil Aviation Rules for aircraft operations
Part 91 covers: airworthiness, flight rules, communication procedures, emergency protocols
Pilot responsibility: compliance with applicable rules; violations subject to enforcement action

Rules structured by subsection: 91.217 (radio), 91.243 (listening watch), 91.245 (comms), 91.247 (position reports), 91.249 (transponder), 91.513 (ELT), 91.515 (post-landing ELT), 91.529 (radio failure)
Study each rule with practical application in mind; understand rationale for safe operations
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Demonstrate a good working knowledge of the following Civil Aviation Rules: Part 91.217 (5)
concept: CAR Part 91.217(5) deals with radio equipment requirements for controlled airspace. It says you must have a functioning radio—specifically, two-way radio communication capability—to operate in controlled airspace. That means your radio must be able to both transmit and receive. If you've only got receive, that doesn't count. If the radio's broken, you can't go into controlled airspace legally. Now, if your radio fails during flight in controlled airspace, you've got procedures to follow (we'll cover that in Part 91.529), but before you depart, your radio must work both ways. When you're doing your preflight and you test the radio, you're confirming compliance with this rule. Some VFR flights in uncontrolled airspace might have more relaxed requirements, but in controlled airspace, it's non-negotiable. The rule recognizes that control depends on communication—if you can't hear ATC or ATC can't hear you, you're a safety hazard in controlled airspace. So before every flight into controlled airspace, make sure your radio passes that two-way test on the ground.
exam_tip: CAR 91.217(5): two-way radio required for controlled airspace operations.
key_facts: CAR Part 91.217(5): aircraft operating in controlled airspace must have functioning two-way radio
Two-way capability: both transmit and receive must be operational; receive-only is insufficient
Pre-flight verification: radio check before flight confirms compliance
Failure during flight: follow CAR 91.529 (radio failure) procedures; squawk 7600
Uncontrolled airspace: may have different radio requirements; check relevant rules
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Demonstrate a good working knowledge of the following Civil Aviation Rules: Part 91.243
concept: CAR Part 91.243 is about listening watch—keeping your radio on and monitoring the frequency. Basically, it says you must maintain a listening watch on the appropriate ATC frequency whenever you're in controlled airspace. This means your radio's on, you're tuned to the right frequency, your volume's loud enough to hear transmissions, and you're actually listening. You can't turn the radio down to a mumble and hope you catch something. You can't switch to a different frequency and forget to switch back. Listening watch isn't passive—it's active attention. You need to hear ATC's calls, other aircraft's transmissions, and any emergency declarations. If you're reading a magazine and not paying attention to the radio, you're not maintaining a listening watch. The rule exists because communication depends on it. If you don't hear an instruction or a warning from another aircraft, you can't respond appropriately. So maintain listening watch means radio on, correct frequency, volume up, and your attention on the frequency unless you're in a brief moment of workload like landing or emergency procedures. It's one of those rules that seems obvious but pilots occasionally skip, especially on long flights when fatigue sets in.
exam_tip: CAR 91.243: maintain listening watch on correct frequency in controlled airspace.
key_facts: CAR Part 91.243: pilot must maintain listening watch on designated ATC frequency
Listening watch: radio on, correct frequency, volume adequate, active attention to transmissions
Requirement applies in controlled airspace; frequency determined by ATC or charts
Fatigue consideration: maintain attention especially on long flights; brief volume checks
Failure to listen: safety risk; miss ATC instructions, traffic warnings, emergency broadcasts
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Demonstrate a good working knowledge of the following Civil Aviation Rules: Part 91.245 (b), (c) and (d)
concept: CAR Part 91.245 covers communication procedures in detail. Section (b) says you must follow standard radio telephony procedures—no ad-hoc communication. You use the phraseology we've been discussing. Section (c) is about not transmitting anything false or misleading on aviation frequencies. That means no jokes, no false emergency declarations, no false position reports. A false Mayday call is a serious criminal offense. Section (d) deals with unnecessary transmissions. You don't clutter the frequency with chatter. Each transmission should have a purpose. You don't say 'Hello Christchurch, how's your day?' You say 'Christchurch Information, Zulu Kilo Bravo Charlie, request QNH'. These three sections basically say: use standard procedures, be truthful, and don't waste frequency time. They're all about professionalism and discipline. If you follow the rules we've been learning about—standard phraseology, professional demeanor, essential information only—you're complying with Part 91.245 automatically. It's the discipline of professional radio operation.
exam_tip: CAR 91.245: standard phraseology only; no false/misleading transmissions; no unnecessary transmissions.
key_facts: CAR 91.245(b): use standard radio telephony procedures and prescribed phraseology
CAR 91.245(c): must not transmit false or misleading signals on aviation frequencies
CAR 91.245(d): avoid unnecessary transmissions; clutter affects safety and efficiency
False emergency: serious offense; false Mayday calls waste SAR resources and risk lives
Professional discipline: standard phrases, truthful content, essential information only
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Demonstrate a good working knowledge of the following Civil Aviation Rules: Part 91.247
concept: CAR Part 91.247 requires position reporting in specific circumstances. The rule is pretty prescriptive about when and where and how often. Generally, you report your position when you enter certain airspace, at designated waypoints, or when ATC requests it. Position reports contain your callsign, your position, your altitude, your heading if relevant, and sometimes your speed. The rule's in place so ATC knows where you are and can maintain separation. It's also a safety backup—if you stop reporting and don't respond to calls, ATC knows something's wrong and can initiate a search. In uncontrolled airspace, you might be required to broadcast position reports at certain points for traffic awareness. In controlled airspace, ATC directs your reports. The exam might ask you to explain when position reports are required or what should be included. The key is: when you're in airspace where the rule applies, you report your position as required. Check the AIP and any specific instructions ATC gives you. Position reports aren't optional—they're a mandatory safety procedure.
exam_tip: CAR 91.247: position reports required in certain airspace; include callsign, position, altitude.
key_facts: CAR Part 91.247: position reporting required in designated airspace and at specified points
Controlled airspace: ATC directs when to report (e.g., 'Report overhead Christchurch')
Uncontrolled airspace: broadcast position at designated waypoints or time intervals
Report content: callsign, position (lat/long or aerodrome reference), altitude, heading if requested
Safety function: enables ATC separation and allows search initiation if no contact
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Demonstrate a good working knowledge of the following Civil Aviation Rules: Part 91.249 (a) and (b)
concept: CAR Part 91.249 covers transponder operation and modes. Section (a) identifies where transponders are mandatory. In NZ, there are transponder mandatory airspace areas—basically controlled airspace and certain terminal areas. If you're in those areas, you must have a transponder and it must be operating. Section (b) specifies which mode—usually Mode C, which sends your altitude and code. The rule's safety-critical because ATC depends on that altitude data for separation. If your transponder's unserviceable and you're supposed to be in transponder mandatory airspace, you can't legally be there unless you've got a special authorization from ATC. We covered earlier what to do if your transponder fails—you declare it and request routing that avoids the mandatory area. The rule enforces that discipline. So before you fly into controlled airspace, check your transponder, select Mode C, and verify it's working via the test function. The exam might ask you to identify transponder mandatory airspace or explain what to do if your transponder's broken. Know this rule because it's fundamental to NZ operations.
exam_tip: CAR 91.249(a)(b): transponder mandatory in controlled/terminal airspace; Mode C required.
key_facts: CAR Part 91.249(a): transponder mandatory in designated airspace (controlled/terminal areas)
CAR Part 91.249(b): Mode C operation required (code + altitude transmission)
Transponder mandatory airspace: identified in AIP and on sectional charts
Unserviceable transponder: uncontrolled airspace okay; mandatory airspace requires ATC authorization for alternative routing
Pilot responsibility: verify transponder operation before entering mandatory airspace
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Demonstrate a good working knowledge of the following Civil Aviation Rules: Part 91.513
concept: CAR Part 91.513 is all about Emergency Locator Transmitter (ELT) requirements for carriage. It states that aircraft operating over water or in remote terrain must carry a serviceable ELT. The ELT must be installed and maintained according to the manufacturer's specifications. It must be registered with the Aeronautical Information Service—that registration includes your aircraft's registration and other identifying data. The rule's straightforward: if you're flying a route that takes you over water or remote area, you need an ELT. Most training aircraft have one permanently installed, so compliance is built-in. But you need to verify it's serviceable—check the service placard, ensure the battery's not dead, make sure the device looks physically intact. If the ELT's overdue for service or the battery's expired, you can't legally operate over water or remote terrain until it's serviced. For the exam, you need to know when ELT carriage is required (over water or remote terrain), and that it must be registered and maintained. These aren't optional—they're mandatory for NZ operations on certain routes.
exam_tip: CAR 91.513: ELT required over water/remote terrain; must be registered and serviceable.
key_facts: CAR Part 91.513: ELT carriage mandatory for flight over water or remote terrain
ELT must be registered via AIS with aircraft registration and identification data
ELT maintenance: periodic servicing required; check service placards and battery expiry
Unserviceable ELT: cannot legally operate over water/remote unless repaired
Preflight: verify ELT installed, within service interval, and registration current
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Demonstrate a good working knowledge of the following Civil Aviation Rules: Part 91.515
concept: CAR Part 91.515 specifies what you must do with the ELT after a forced landing. The rule says: after landing as a result of an emergency, you must ensure the ELT is operating and continues to operate until directed otherwise by rescue authorities. Basically, if you've crashed or made a forced landing, the ELT should be transmitting and you leave it transmitting. Don't switch it off unless the rescue crew physically tells you to. The 406 MHz signal's already been detected by satellite, and the 121.5 MHz signal's helping rescuers home in. Your job post-crash is to stay with the aircraft, keep the ELT running, and wait for rescue. The rule protects you by ensuring continuous transmission of your location. Under this rule, you might have manually activated the ELT if the g-switch didn't work, or you're letting the g-switch's automatic activation keep running. Either way, the ELT keeps transmitting until rescuers arrive and tell you to stop. It's a simple rule with one message: after emergency landing, ELT stays on. That's it.
exam_tip: CAR 91.515: after forced landing, maintain ELT transmission until directed to stop by rescue authorities.
