key_facts: CAR Part 91.515: ELT must be operating after forced landing due to emergency
Transmission maintained until rescuers direct otherwise; do not switch off prematurely
Stay with aircraft; 406 MHz signal already detected; 121.5 MHz aids location
Make aircraft visible; conserve battery if possible, but maintain transmission
Coordinate with rescue via radio if able; follow their instructions regarding ELT
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Demonstrate a good working knowledge of the following Civil Aviation Rules: Part 91.529
concept: CAR Part 91.529 is the radio failure rule. It tells you what to do if your radio dies during flight in controlled airspace. First: squawk 7600 on your transponder. That tells ATC on radar that you've lost comms—they'll see your blip and the 7600 code. Second: comply with the last clearance you received from ATC. If they cleared you to 3000 feet on a specific heading, maintain that heading and altitude. Don't deviate unless safety requires it. Third: listen for light signals from ATC. If you're near an aerodrome with a tower and you can't hear them, they might use a spotlight to give you instructions. Steady green means you're cleared to land or takeoff; steady red means give way or stop; green flashes mean return for landing; red flashes mean aerodrome unsafe. Fourth: try to re-establish contact. Switch to standby frequency (typically 121.5 MHz) and attempt a transmission. Try VHF frequencies sequentially. If you get comms back, great. If not, continue to the nearest suitable aerodrome and land as soon as possible. The rule's designed to keep you safe and keep other traffic safe while you're unable to communicate. It's orderly and sequential—squawk 7600, follow last clearance, watch for lights, land.
exam_tip: CAR 91.529: radio failure = squawk 7600, comply with last clearance, watch for light signals, land nearest aerodrome.
key_facts: CAR Part 91.529: radio failure procedure in controlled airspace
Step 1: Squawk 7600 (transponder code alerts ATC to lost comms)
Step 2: Comply with last clearance received (maintain altitude/heading)
Step 3: Watch for light signals from tower (green = clear, red = stop)
Step 4: Land at nearest suitable aerodrome; attempt radio on alternative frequencies
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: AIPNZ Volume 1, describe the radio procedures, requirements, and functions associated with: UNICOM
concept: UNICOM is essentially a ground-based advisory service you'll find at unattended aerodromes. While it's not a formal ATS (Air Traffic Service), it can be really handy for getting runway and weather information. The thing is, it's entirely advisory — they're not giving you clearances or control instructions. Think of it as a helpful mate on the ground who's keeping an eye on the aerodrome and can tell you what's happening. You'll use UNICOM mainly at places like Ardmore or smaller fields where there's no AFIS service. When you call, remember you're still the pilot in command making the final decisions about landing, takeoff, and taxiing. UNICOM will give you info about active runway, wind conditions, and other aircraft in the circuit, but it's your responsibility to integrate that information and fly safely. It's a useful service, but don't confuse it with actual ATC — you're always responsible for your own separation and traffic awareness.
exam_tip: UNICOM is advisory only — information, not clearances.
key_facts: UNICOM provides aerodrome advisory information at unattended aerodromes
UNICOM is NOT an ATS and does not issue clearances or instructions
You remain pilot in command and responsible for all decisions
Use UNICOM for runway status, wind, and traffic information
Typically found at smaller NZ aerodromes like Ardmore and Matamata
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: AIPNZ Volume 1, describe the radio procedures, requirements, and functions associated with: ATIS
concept: ATIS (Automatic Terminal Information Service) is a continuous recorded broadcast of aerodrome information at busy controlled aerodromes. You'll hear it at places like Auckland, Christchurch, or Wellington Tower. The beauty of ATIS is that you can listen to it before you call up the tower — it saves airtime because you've already got the essential data. ATIS gives you runway in use, wind, visibility, QNH, and any significant aerodrome conditions. One important thing: when you listen to ATIS, you'll hear an information designation (usually a letter like 'Bravo' or 'Delta'). When you call the tower, you'll tell them you've received that particular ATIS, so they know you're up to speed. This saves them repeating all that stuff. It's straightforward, but it's a real efficiency tool — use it properly and you look professional.
exam_tip: Listen to ATIS before calling the tower; quote the information letter when you call.
key_facts: ATIS is a continuous recorded broadcast at controlled aerodromes
Provides runway, wind, visibility, QNH, and aerodrome conditions
Each ATIS broadcast has a letter designation (e.g., Bravo, Delta)
Quote the ATIS information letter when calling the tower
Saves tower airtime by giving you essential data in advance
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: AIPNZ Volume 1, describe the radio procedures, requirements, and functions associated with: AFRU
concept: AFRU (Aerodrome Frequency Response Unit) is a service you might encounter at certain unattended aerodromes. It's essentially a backup system that can relay information when there's no formal ATS on duty. If AFRU is active at the field you're planning to visit, you can use it to get runway and aerodrome information. Like UNICOM, it's advisory only — they're not controlling traffic or issuing clearances. AFRU is often combined with other services, so check your AIPNZ carefully before you fly. The key thing is knowing what services are available at your destination. This is why pre-flight planning is crucial: dig into the AIPNZ, work out whether you're going to a controlled aerodrome, AFIS, or unattended field with UNICOM or AFRU. Different services mean different procedures, and you need to know what you're dealing with before you're airborne.
exam_tip: AFRU is advisory-only at unattended aerodromes; check AIPNZ before you go.
key_facts: AFRU provides advisory information at some unattended aerodromes
No clearances or control; information-only service
Often combined with other aerodrome services
Check AIPNZ Volume 1 for availability at your destination
Pilot remains responsible for all aerodrome operations decisions
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: AIPNZ Volume 1, describe the radio procedures, requirements, and functions associated with: AWIB
concept: AWIB (Aerodrome Weather Information Broadcast) is a recorded broadcast of weather information specific to that aerodrome. You'll find it at some of the larger NZ aerodromes, and it's super useful because you get dedicated weather data without tying up a frequency or waiting for a controller to read it to you. AWIB includes things like wind, visibility, cloud, temperature, dew point, and any weather phenomena affecting the aerodrome. Think of it as a weather-only version of ATIS — it's automated, always available, and gives you real-time aerodrome weather. Before you call the tower or land, tune in to AWIB and get the latest picture. It's especially handy if you're deciding whether to divert or whether your alternate is going to be viable. The information's current and specific to that location, not a broad regional TAF. Get into the habit of grabbing AWIB before you contact ATC.
exam_tip: AWIB = aerodrome weather only; listen before calling the tower.
key_facts: AWIB broadcasts recorded aerodrome-specific weather information
Includes wind, visibility, cloud, temperature, and dew point
Available continuously at participating aerodromes
Different from regional TAF — specific to that location
Use AWIB to make weather-related decisions before contacting ATC
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: AIPNZ demonstrate a good working knowledge of the phraseology used for, and by, VFR aircraft.
concept: VFR phraseology is your bread and butter as a light aircraft pilot in New Zealand. It's different from IFR phraseology, so you need to be crystal clear on what you're saying and what controllers are telling you. The standard words matter — you're not being pedantic when you use AFFIRM instead of 'yes' or NEGATIVE instead of 'no'. That clarity keeps everyone on the same page, especially when things get busy in the circuit at Auckland or Christchurch. You'll use specific phrases for different situations: requesting taxi, calling for approach, reading back instructions. Get comfortable with it now during training because when you're concentrating on flying the aircraft in turbulence or on approach to a new field, you don't want to be thinking about what to say. Practice the standard phraseology repeatedly — say it out loud, get it ingrained. Your instructor will drill this with you because it's fundamental to safe flight operations. Good phraseology = good communication = safe flying.
exam_tip: Use standard VFR phraseology consistently; clarity saves lives.
key_facts: VFR phraseology uses standard words: AFFIRM, NEGATIVE, ROGER, WILCO
Different from IFR phraseology; know which context you're in
Standard phrases reduce misunderstanding and save airtime
Practice out loud until phraseology becomes automatic
Consistent phraseology is part of professional flight operations
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Demonstrate a good working knowledge of the AIPNZ and AC172-1 with regard to: Communication services
concept: Communication services in NZ airspace range from full ATC services at major aerodromes to basic UNICOM at small strips. You need to know what you're getting at each place. At controlled aerodromes like Auckland, Wellington, or Christchurch, you're getting full ATC — clearances, instructions, separation from other traffic. At AFIS aerodromes like Hamilton or Palmerston North, you're getting flight information but not ATC clearances — that's an important distinction. The controller's giving you info to help you make your own decisions, not telling you what to do. Then there's unattended aerodromes where you're broadcasting your intentions on CTAF. Each service type requires different phraseology and procedures, so knowing what's available before you fly is essential for good planning. Get to know the NZ system — know which aerodromes are controlled, which have AFIS, which are unattended. It'll make you a more confident, safer pilot.
exam_tip: NZ has three aerodrome types: Controlled, AFIS, and Unattended — know the differences.
key_facts: Controlled aerodromes: full ATC with clearances and instructions
AFIS aerodromes: information service, not clearances (Hamilton, Palmerston North)
Unattended aerodromes: pilot broadcasts intentions on CTAF (Ardmore, Matamata)
Each service type requires different phraseology and procedures
Pre-flight planning must include identifying aerodrome type
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Demonstrate a good working knowledge of the AIPNZ and AC172-1 with regard to: Communication aspects in the Search and Rescue section
concept: Search and Rescue (SAR) communication is a serious part of aviation in New Zealand. If you're in distress or urgency, you're communicating with SAR authorities through Airways NZ or other ATS. Understanding the SAR system means knowing how to alert them, what information they need, and how you're going to stay in contact. When you transmit MAYDAY or PAN PAN, you're essentially notifying SAR that you need help. The Rescue Coordination Centre will be coordinated through your position reports and communications. That's why your position and intentions need to be accurate — SAR is going to come looking for you based on what you tell them. You also need to understand that other aircraft hearing a MAYDAY will pass it on if you're not heard directly. The SAR system relies on good communication protocols and precise information. Know the distress frequencies, know how to format a distress call, and know what SAR will need from you. It's not something you think about every flight, but understanding it could be critical one day.
exam_tip: SAR depends on accurate position, type of aircraft, POB, and intentions; transmit clearly.
key_facts: SAR is coordinated through Airways NZ Rescue Coordination Centre
MAYDAY and PAN PAN alerts activate SAR response procedures
Accurate position, aircraft type, and POB are essential for SAR
Other aircraft relay distress calls if the aircraft isn't heard directly
SAR communication requires precision and standard phraseology
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Demonstrate proficiency in standard radiotelephony phraseologies and procedures for:
concept: Proficiency in standard radiotelephony is what separates professional pilots from 'just getting by' operators. It's not enough to kind of understand what someone's saying — you need to be able to transmit and receive with clarity and consistency. You'll use specific phraseology for every phase of flight: from your initial contact with the station to taxi, takeoff, climb, approach, and landing. Each aerodrome type (controlled, AFIS, unattended) has its own procedures and call formats. What works at Auckland Tower won't work at Ardmore. That's why we're drilling this throughout your training. You'll practice these procedures repeatedly until they're muscle memory. In the real world, when you're concentrating on flying the aircraft, you don't want to be thinking about what to say next. The phraseology should flow naturally because you've practiced it so much. It's like learning to drive — initially you think about every action, but eventually it becomes second nature. That's the level of proficiency we're aiming for here.
exam_tip: Proficiency = muscle memory; practice until phraseology is automatic.
key_facts: Standard phraseology must be used consistently across all flight phases
Different procedures for controlled, AFIS, and unattended aerodromes
Practice until phraseology becomes automatic, not conscious
Clarity and consistency reduce misunderstanding and workload
Professional communication is part of professional flying
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Demonstrate proficiency in standard radiotelephony phraseologies and procedures for: All VFR operations in controlled and uncontrolled airspace
concept: Flying VFR means you've got flexibility, but that doesn't mean you get to make up your own radio procedures. Whether you're in controlled airspace (like around Auckland) or uncontrolled airspace (like heading out towards a small unattended field), you need to know the standard phraseology for VFR operations. In controlled airspace, you're talking to ATC and following their instructions — they're managing your separation from other traffic. You need to know how to request clearance, acknowledge instructions, and read back what they've told you. In uncontrolled airspace, you're broadcasting your position and intentions so everyone else can see the traffic picture. You're not getting instructions, you're sharing information. The phraseology might look similar, but the context is different. Get comfortable with both because your VFR operations will take you through both types of airspace. Practice the standard calls until they're second nature — your first call to a new aerodrome shouldn't be a surprise.
exam_tip: Controlled airspace = ATC instructions; uncontrolled = broadcasts of position and intentions.
key_facts: Controlled airspace requires ATC clearances and instruction compliance
Uncontrolled airspace requires position and intention broadcasts
VFR phraseology is consistent but context changes your responsibility
Standard calls for each phase: initial contact, climb, descent, approach
Know your local airspace structure before flying
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Demonstrate proficiency in standard radiotelephony phraseologies and procedures for: Taxi, take-off, approach and landing at controlled aerodromes, Flight Service aerodromes, aerodromes served by UNICOM, and uncontrolled aerodromes
concept: The phases of flight where you're on or near the aerodrome are critical, and the procedures are different depending on whether you're at a controlled aerodrome like Christchurch, an AFIS aerodrome like Hamilton, or an unattended field like Ardmore. At a controlled aerodrome, you're requesting taxi clearance, getting a takeoff clearance from the tower, and requesting approach clearance when you're inbound. They're managing your traffic flow. At AFIS, you're telling them your intentions, they're giving you information, and you're making your own decision to taxi and takeoff. At unattended fields, you're broadcasting 'Traffic Ardmore' with your position and intentions. The landing procedures follow the same pattern — the aerodrome type determines who you're talking to and what you need to do. Nail these procedures because this is where most incidents happen: in the circuit, on approach, on takeoff. You'll practice each aerodrome type during training. Know them cold.
exam_tip: Aerodrome type determines procedure: Controlled = clearances; AFIS = info; Unattended = broadcasts.
key_facts: Controlled: request taxi, await clearance, read back runway and QNH
AFIS: inform of intentions, get weather/runway info, make own decisions
Unattended: broadcast 'Traffic [name]' with position, level, intentions
Landing procedures follow the same aerodrome-type logic
Practice each aerodrome type until procedures are automatic
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Demonstrate proficiency in standard radiotelephony phraseologies and procedures for: Read-back instructions
concept: Readback is your safety net. When ATC gives you an instruction that's critical — like a runway, an altitude, a heading, a speed, or a code — you need to read it back to confirm you've understood it correctly. This stops misunderstandings from turning into incidents. The readback rule is straightforward: if it's a clearance or instruction, you read it back. If ATC gives you the runway, you say back the runway. If they give you an altitude to maintain, you say that altitude back. If they give you a squawk code, you read that back too. The tower or approach controller will then confirm your readback is correct or correct you if you've got it wrong. It might seem repetitive, but it works. I've seen pilots miss critical instructions because they weren't paying attention or misheard — readback catches those errors before they become problems. Get into the habit now: listen, confirm, read back. It's a professional habit that keeps you safe.
exam_tip: Readback clearances and instructions to confirm understanding; ATC will correct you if wrong.
key_facts: Readback required for runway, altitude, heading, speed, SSR code, clearances
Prevents misunderstandings from becoming incidents
ATC will confirm your readback is correct or correct any errors
Standard readback format: repeat the information you received
Readback is part of every professional flight operation
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: State the limitations on pilots with regard to: Unauthorised transmissions
concept: This is straightforward: you're not allowed to transmit on aviation frequencies without authorization, and you're definitely not allowed to transmit false or misleading information. That means no joking around on the frequency, no transmitting messages that aren't related to the flight, and no making transmissions to test your radio or 'see if it works'. Every transmission ties up airtime and can disrupt important communications. If you make an unauthorized transmission, you're not only breaking the rules, you could be interfering with someone else's safety-critical communication. That's serious. Keep transmissions brief and relevant to the flight — ATC will actually appreciate it. Don't chat, don't transmit unnecessary information, don't test your radio on a live frequency. It's a simple rule, but it's there for safety. Keep the frequency clear for essential communications.
exam_tip: No unauthorized transmissions, no false info, no joking around on the frequency.
key_facts: Unauthorized transmissions are against the rules and potentially dangerous
False or misleading information is prohibited
Unnecessary transmissions tie up critical airtime
All transmissions must be related to the flight
Keep transmissions brief and professional
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: State the limitations on pilots with regard to: Secrecy of communications
concept: When you're flying, you're hearing all sorts of radio traffic — other pilots' positions, ATC instructions, weather information — and some of it might be sensitive information. You need to keep that information confidential. You can't go around telling people what you overheard on the frequency or using information from aviation communications for purposes they weren't intended for. This is a legal obligation, not just an ethical one. If you overhear something commercially sensitive or that affects someone's safety or privacy, you don't go sharing it with the world. The same applies if you're operating in airspace where there's security-sensitive activity — you're not broadcasting details of military operations or special operations. It's a trust thing: the aviation community relies on everyone respecting the confidentiality of communications. Do your part by keeping what you hear on the radio to yourself unless there's a clear safety reason to share it.
exam_tip: Information from aviation frequencies is confidential; don't divulge it to unauthorised persons.
key_facts: Secrecy of communications is a legal obligation
Don't share radio traffic information with unauthorized persons
Keep sensitive or security-related information confidential
Applies to all information heard on aviation frequencies
Trust and confidentiality are essential to the aviation system
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Controlled aerodrome phraseology
concept: At a controlled aerodrome like Christchurch, Wellington, Auckland, or Queenstown, you're dealing with ATC who's managing traffic flow and separation. The phraseology is formal and follows a standard structure. Your initial call will be something like 'Christchurch Tower, Zulu Kilo Alpha Bravo Charlie, Cessna 172, request taxi for VFR departure to the north.' You state the tower, your callsign, aircraft type, and what you want. From there, they'll either give you a clearance or tell you to wait. When they give you information — runway in use, QNH, surface wind — you read it back. If they clear you for takeoff, you acknowledge and confirm. The language is precise and professional. You're not chatty, you're not uncertain, you're concise. Everyone on the frequency needs to understand what's happening, and standard phraseology makes that possible. Practice this format repeatedly because every controlled aerodrome works this way, and consistency means you won't trip up at a new field.
exam_tip: Controlled aerodrome: '[Tower name], [callsign], [type], [request]' — wait for instructions.
key_facts: Call format: '[Station] Tower, [callsign], [type], [message]'
Always get clearance before taxi at controlled aerodromes
Read back runway, QNH, and all ATC instructions
Acknowledge and confirm takeoff clearance
Professional, concise phraseology at all times
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: AFIS aerodrome phraseology
concept: AFIS aerodromes like Hamilton or Palmerston North are different from controlled aerodromes, and you need to understand that difference in how you communicate. AFIS gives you information — runway in use, surface wind, visibility, circuit conditions — but they're NOT giving you control instructions or clearances. You're making your own decisions based on the information they provide. Your call format is 'Hamilton Flight Service, [callsign], [message]' — and you're informing them of your intentions, not requesting permission. You might say 'request runway information for landing' or 'inbound for landing.' They'll give you the runway, wind, and relevant information. You then make your own decision to land or hold off. If there's other traffic, AFIS will tell you about it, but they won't tell you to give way — that's your decision as pilot in command. The key difference from a controlled aerodrome is that you're not getting clearances; you're getting information and making your own calls. That's a bigger workload for you as the pilot, so be aware of it.
exam_tip: AFIS gives information, NOT clearances. You make own decisions; they inform you of traffic.
key_facts: Call format: '[Station] Flight Service, [callsign], [message]'
AFIS provides information; you make your own decisions
Runway information, wind, and traffic info are advisory
No clearances required for taxi, takeoff, or landing at AFIS aerodromes
You retain full responsibility for traffic separation
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Unattended aerodrome phraseology
concept: At unattended aerodromes like Ardmore, Matamata, or Whitianga, there's no controller or flight service — you're broadcasting to anyone listening on CTAF. The phraseology for unattended aerodromes uses 'Traffic [aerodrome name]' as your opening. For example, at Ardmore you'd say 'Traffic Ardmore, [callsign], Cessna 172, downwind for runway 28.' You're announcing your position and intentions so everyone in the circuit — other aircraft, people on the ground — knows what you're doing. This is a fundamental safety thing: you're sharing the circuit with other pilots who might not be in radio contact with each other, so you're relying on radio calls to build the traffic picture. Make your calls clear and at the right positions: on base, on downwind, on final, and over the runway threshold. As you get more experienced, you'll build a mental picture of where other aircraft are in the circuit based on their radio calls. Never assume the frequency is monitored or that everyone hears you, so always maintain visual scan and use your eyes as your primary collision avoidance system.
exam_tip: Unattended: 'Traffic [name], [callsign], [position/intention]' — share the traffic picture.
key_facts: Call format: 'Traffic [aerodrome name], [callsign], [message]'
Used at unattended aerodromes (Ardmore, Matamata, Whitianga)
Announce position: downwind, base, final, over threshold
Share traffic picture; others rely on your position reports
Visual scan is primary; radio calls are secondary collision avoidance
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: MBZ (Mandatory Broadcast Zone) procedures
concept: MBZ is an acronym you need to know: Mandatory Broadcast Zone. Around certain unattended aerodromes in New Zealand, there's an MBZ — an airspace volume within which you're required to broadcast your position and intentions. The MBZ is typically defined in the AIPNZ and extends from the surface up to a specified altitude (often 1500 feet or 2000 feet) and covers a certain radius around the aerodrome. If you're flying through an MBZ, you must make position broadcasts on CTAF, even if you're just passing through and not planning to land. The MBZ exists because the aerodrome is unattended and there's no ATS, so the radio broadcasts are the only way to share the traffic picture. When you're in the MBZ, you're applying the unattended aerodrome procedures — calling 'Traffic [name]' with your position and intentions. Check your flight planning to identify any MBZs on your route and know the frequencies. It's a straightforward system, but you need to know the rules.
exam_tip: MBZ = Mandatory Broadcast Zone; broadcasts required within MBZ even if not landing.
key_facts: MBZ is defined in AIPNZ for designated unattended aerodromes
Broadcasts mandatory within MBZ regardless of landing intention
Typical MBZ extends 2000 feet AGL and 2-5 nm radius
Use CTAF frequency and 'Traffic [name]' format
Check AIPNZ during flight planning to identify MBZs on your route
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Readback requirements for ATC clearances
concept: When ATC gives you a clearance, you've got to read back certain items to confirm you've understood them correctly. This is mandatory at controlled aerodromes, and it's your safety net. The items that require readback are: runway in use, altimeter setting (QNH), SSR code (transponder squawk), any level instruction (altitude or flight level), heading instruction, speed instruction, any airways clearance, approach clearance, and takeoff/landing clearance. Essentially, if it's a control instruction or something that affects your flight path or equipment, it needs to be read back. The readback format is straightforward: you repeat back what they told you. For example, if they say 'Cessna 172, clear for takeoff, runway 27,' you'd readback 'Zulu Kilo Alpha Bravo Charlie, clear for takeoff runway 27.' ATC will then confirm your readback is correct. If you get something wrong in your readback, they'll correct you immediately. This system catches errors before they become problems — it's a critical safety procedure.
exam_tip: Readback: runway, QNH, SSR, altitude, heading, speed, and all ATC control instructions.
key_facts: Readback required for all control instructions and clearances
Must confirm: runway, QNH, SSR code, altitude, heading, speed
Format: repeat back the instruction word-for-word
ATC confirms your readback or corrects you immediately
Readback is mandatory safety procedure at controlled aerodromes
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Describe the degrees of emergency that warrant: A distress call (MAYDAY)
concept: MAYDAY is what you transmit when you're in grave and imminent danger — when there's an immediate threat to life or the aircraft. Think structural failure, complete engine failure with no landing options, fire in the cockpit, serious injury to a crew member that prevents continued safe flight. You're not saying MAYDAY lightly or just because you're having a rough time. It's a specific level of emergency: you're in serious trouble right now and you need immediate help. When you transmit MAYDAY, everyone on the frequency needs to recognize that you're in grave danger and that this takes absolute priority. All other traffic clears the frequency to listen. It's about communicating the severity of your situation clearly. If you're not in grave and imminent danger but you've got an urgent situation — say, an engine problem but you can still reach an aerodrome, or a medical emergency but the patient is stable — that's PAN PAN, not MAYDAY. Get the level right. Declaring MAYDAY when you're not in grave danger wastes emergency resources. Knowing the difference is important.
exam_tip: MAYDAY = grave and imminent danger; life or aircraft immediately at risk.
key_facts: MAYDAY declared when grave and imminent danger exists
Immediate threat to life or loss of aircraft
Examples: structural failure, complete engine loss, fire, serious injury
All other traffic clears frequency to listen
Not to be declared lightly or unnecessarily
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Describe the degrees of emergency that warrant: An urgency call (PAN PAN)
concept: PAN PAN is your urgency call — it's serious, but it's not grave and imminent danger. You're declaring PAN PAN when you've got an urgent situation that requires immediate help or attention but the aircraft and persons on board aren't in immediate danger. Examples might be engine problems where you can still reach an aerodrome, a medical emergency where the patient is stable but needs medical attention on the ground, structural damage that's not critical, or fuel emergencies where you've got enough to get to the nearest field but it's close. PAN PAN signals 'I need help now, but I'm not about to fall out of the sky.' The difference between MAYDAY and PAN PAN matters. With PAN PAN, other aircraft can continue their flight once they know you're being handled, whereas MAYDAY clears the frequency entirely. The level of response also differs — MAYDAY gets you SAR assets, fire trucks, and full emergency response. PAN PAN gets you priority but a more measured response. Know the difference and declare the right one.
exam_tip: PAN PAN = urgent situation requiring immediate help, but no grave and imminent danger.
key_facts: PAN PAN declared for urgent but non-critical emergencies
Examples: engine problems, medical emergency, fuel uncertainty, structural damage
Aircraft and crew not in immediate danger
Receives priority handling but lower response level than MAYDAY
Other aircraft continue flight once PAN PAN situation is understood
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Explain the procedures and phraseology involved in transmitting a MAYDAY and PAN call with emphasis on: Radio frequencies
concept: When you're in distress, you want to transmit where people can hear you. Start on your current frequency — if you're in contact with ATC, they're the best positioned to coordinate help. Keep transmitting on your working frequency as long as there's a chance someone's hearing you. If you don't get a response or you're not sure anyone's heard, switch to 121.5 MHz, the international emergency frequency. It's monitored by Airways NZ and lots of other aircraft. 121.5 gets you the widest audience but your current ATC frequency is already connecting you to someone who can help coordinate the response. The rule is simple: transmit on the frequency where you're likely to be heard. If you're in an uncontrolled area and not in contact with anyone, go straight to 121.5. If you're near an aerodrome or in controlled airspace, your working frequency might be better because ATC is already tracking you. Use your judgment based on where you are and who can hear you. Once SAR has you, they'll direct your transmissions.
exam_tip: Distress: use working frequency first, then 121.5 MHz if no response.
key_facts: Transmit on current frequency first (ATC is already tracking you)
If no response, switch to 121.5 MHz (international emergency frequency)
121.5 monitored by Airways NZ and other aircraft
Goal is to be heard by someone who can coordinate help
Stay on the frequency where you're being heard and tracked
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Explain the procedures and phraseology involved in transmitting a MAYDAY and PAN call with emphasis on: Station(s) to call
concept: When you're transmitting MAYDAY or PAN PAN, you're calling any station that can hear you — primarily ATC or Airways, but potentially other aircraft. You don't need to call a specific station; you're transmitting 'MAYDAY MAYDAY MAYDAY' or 'PAN PAN PAN PAN PAN PAN' followed by your callsign and aircraft type. Whoever hears you will respond. If you're in controlled airspace, your ATC facility is the primary listener and will coordinate SAR. If you're in uncontrolled airspace or out of range of ATC, other aircraft might hear you and relay your position to Airways or other emergency services. The key is getting the distress or urgency call out; the station monitoring your frequency will pick it up and handle it. Don't worry about calling the 'right' station — get the distress call out clearly and let the network handle the coordination. Airways NZ and other trained listeners will have it covered. Your job is to transmit clearly and get your information out.
exam_tip: Transmit 'MAYDAY/PAN PAN' to any station; ATC coordinates response.
key_facts: Distress calls reach any monitoring station: ATC, Airways, other aircraft
No need to call a specific station; broadcast to all
ATC (if in range) will coordinate SAR response
Other aircraft may relay if you're out of ATC range
Clarity and correct phraseology ensure message is understood
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Explain the procedures and phraseology involved in transmitting a MAYDAY and PAN call with emphasis on: Contents of the call
concept: Your distress call needs to convey specific information so SAR and ATC know what they're dealing with and can mount an appropriate response. The format is: MAYDAY (or PAN PAN) repeated three times, then your callsign and aircraft type, the nature of the distress or urgency, your intentions, your position, altitude, and heading, number of people on board, and any other relevant information like fuel remaining. For example: 'MAYDAY MAYDAY MAYDAY, Airways, Zulu Kilo Alpha Bravo Charlie, Cessna 172, engine failure, intending to land at nearest aerodrome, position 5 miles west of Ardmore, 1000 feet, heading 090, 2 souls on board, fuel 20 minutes remaining.' That's concise but packed with the info SAR needs. Your position is critical — use landmarks, distance from aerodromes, or GPS if you're not sure. Your intentions tell them whether you're ditching, forced landing, or continuing to an aerodrome. Number of people and fuel tells them what resources they need and how urgently. Get these details right and SAR can start working on a response immediately.
exam_tip: MAYDAY: [station], [callsign], [nature], [intentions], [position/altitude/heading], [POB], [fuel].
key_facts: Format: MAYDAY/PAN PAN x3, callsign, type, nature of emergency
Include intentions (land, ditch, divert)
State position clearly (landmark, distance from known point, or coordinates)
Include altitude, heading, number of persons on board
Add fuel remaining, injuries, or other critical information
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Explain the procedures and phraseology involved in transmitting a MAYDAY and PAN call with emphasis on: Enforcing radio silence
concept: Once a MAYDAY (or PAN PAN) call is transmitted, radio discipline is crucial. Everyone on that frequency needs to shut up and listen. No unnecessary transmissions, no chatter, no one asking questions. The distressed aircraft needs a clear frequency to communicate with ATS and SAR. If you hear a distress call and you're not involved in the rescue or communication, your job is to listen silently and be ready to relay if needed. Don't clutter the frequency with 'we're coming to help' or other well-intentioned but unhelpful transmissions. If you do need to relay a distress call because the distressed aircraft can't reach ATS directly, you keep it professional and brief. The person in distress might be concentrating on flying the aircraft, managing the emergency, and talking to SAR — they don't need distractions. Radio silence around a distress call shows respect for the seriousness of the situation and helps ensure that critical communications get through. It's part of the professional aviation culture.
exam_tip: MAYDAY declared: all other traffic maintains radio silence; relay only if needed.
key_facts: MAYDAY declared: all other aircraft maintain absolute radio silence
Relay distress calls only if the distressed aircraft can't reach ATS
No unnecessary transmissions or chatter on the frequency
Give the distressed aircraft clear frequency for SAR coordination
Silence shows respect and ensures critical communications get through
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Describe the actions by stations not involved in the emergency with regard to: Radio silence
concept: When you hear a MAYDAY on the frequency, your first action is to shut up and listen. That's it. No transmissions, no questions, no trying to help by relaying unless specifically asked or unless you're sure the distressed aircraft can't reach ATS directly. Radio silence around a distress call is non-negotiable. The frequency needs to be clear for the distressed aircraft to communicate with ATC or SAR. Every transmission from you — even if you think it's helpful — is potentially covering something critical. If you're in a flight training scenario or you hear a test MAYDAY, the same rule applies: silence until the distress traffic is ended. The only exception is if you can actually contribute to the rescue — for instance, if you're nearby and can provide visual assistance or if the distressed aircraft specifically asks for relay. Otherwise, maintain radio silence, listen to what's happening, and be ready to act if called upon. It's a simple rule but it saves lives.
exam_tip: Hear MAYDAY: maintain radio silence; don't transmit unless specifically assisting.
key_facts: Any aircraft hearing MAYDAY maintains absolute radio silence
No transmissions except to assist or relay if specifically needed
Keep frequency clear for distressed aircraft and SAR
Listen and monitor in case you can provide assistance
Silence is your contribution to the emergency response
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Describe the actions by stations not involved in the emergency with regard to: Provision of assistance
concept: If you're nearby and can actually help in a distress situation, that's different. If you hear a MAYDAY and you're in a position to provide visual assistance, relay to ATS, or help coordinate SAR, then you transmit. For example, if a pilot declares MAYDAY and you're close enough to see them ditch or force land, or you can see their position more clearly than they can, that information is valuable. You'd break radio silence specifically to provide that assistance. Similarly, if the distressed aircraft is out of range of ATS but you can hear them and relay to ATS, that's a service you provide. But these actions need to be proportionate and justified — not just 'we want to help so we'll start transmitting.' The golden rule is: only transmit if you're genuinely contributing to the rescue. Don't assume the distressed aircraft needs your help; if they do, they'll ask or ATC will ask you. Otherwise, you listen and stand by. That's how you actually help in an emergency.
exam_tip: Assist only if you can provide genuine help (relay, visual position, specific info).
key_facts: Break radio silence only to provide genuine assistance
Examples: visual position, relay to ATS, specific information about distressed aircraft
Don't transmit unless your information is essential to the rescue
Wait for requests from distressed aircraft or ATC before offering help
Listening and being available is also a form of assistance
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Describe the procedure involved in terminating emergency communications.
concept: A distress or urgency situation doesn't end until someone with authority declares it ended. That authority is the station controlling the emergency — usually ATC or SAR Coordination Centre. When the emergency is resolved, ATC or SAR will say 'DISTRESS TRAFFIC ENDED' or 'PAN TRAFFIC ENDED' on the frequency. That's the signal that the emergency is over and normal frequency operations can resume. You don't get to decide the emergency is finished just because the aircraft landed or the situation seems resolved. There might be post-emergency coordination, follow-up transmissions, or information gathering happening. Let ATC or the controlling authority make the call. Once they declare the traffic ended, normal radio discipline and procedures resume. This is important because people listening on the frequency need clear indication that the emergency is finished so they know they can resume normal operations. The controlled termination also ensures all parties are clear that the emergency response is complete.
exam_tip: DISTRESS/PAN TRAFFIC ENDED: only ATC/SAR can declare emergency over.
key_facts: Emergency communications controlled by ATC or SAR Coordination Centre
DISTRESS TRAFFIC ENDED or PAN TRAFFIC ENDED declares emergency finished
Pilot cannot unilaterally end distress traffic
Normal frequency operations resume after traffic ended is declared
Clear termination ensures all parties know emergency response is complete
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Describe the checks that should be followed when becoming aware of an aircraft communication system failure, including a check of:
concept: You're flying along, and suddenly you can't hear ATC or they're not hearing you. Don't panic. Go through systematic checks. First, check your volume — if it's turned down, that's an easy fix. Next, check the squelch — make sure it's not set too high and blocking signals. Confirm you're on the right frequency — easy mistake, happens more often than you'd think. Check that your headset is plugged in properly — sometimes a loose connector is all it takes. Then try the standby COM radio if you've got one. If nothing works and you've got dual comm radios, switch to the other one. If that doesn't work, try 121.5 MHz — someone might hear you there. These checks are logical: you're ruling out the simplest problems first. Volume, frequency, headset — these are quick wins. Then you move to equipment problems. The point is not to sit there saying 'I can't transmit' without actually investigating what's wrong. Nine times out of ten it's something simple.
exam_tip: Radio failure checks: volume, squelch, frequency, headset, COM2, 121.5.
key_facts: Check volume (not turned down), squelch (not blocking signals)
Confirm correct frequency is selected
Check headset is properly plugged in and working
Try standby/secondary COM radio if available
If no success, try 121.5 MHz or continue with non-voice means
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Describe the checks that should be followed when becoming aware of an aircraft communication system failure, including a check of: Radio power source
concept: Okay, you think your radio's failed. Before you declare communications failure and do all the emergency procedures, make sure it's actually failed. Start with the simple stuff: Is your volume off? Can you hear the background noise on the frequency? If not, turn it up. Is your squelch set so high that it's blocking all signals? Turn it down and try again. Are you on the correct frequency? Double-check that you've selected the right one. Is your headset plugged in? Could there be a loose connector? These things seem obvious, but they're the most common causes of 'radio failure.' Next, if you've got a secondary COM radio, switch to it. Try transmitting on the new radio. If you get through, your primary radio's broken but you've still got comms. If you don't have a secondary radio or switching doesn't work, try 121.5 — a wide area frequency that's monitored everywhere. The key is systematic checking: eliminate the simple problems before you assume genuine equipment failure.
exam_tip: Before declaring failure: check volume, squelch, frequency, headset, try COM2 and 121.5.
key_facts: Volume: check it's turned up and you can hear static
Squelch: adjust if too high, blocking all signals
Frequency: confirm you're on correct frequency
Headset: ensure properly plugged in and functioning
Secondary radio: switch if available and try transmitting
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Describe the checks that should be followed when becoming aware of an aircraft communication system failure, including a check of: Function settings (frequency, squelch and similar)
concept: You've gone through all the checks: volume's up, squelch is fine, you're on the right frequency, headset works, secondary radio doesn't help, and 121.5 produces nothing. Now you've got a genuine radio failure. What do you do? First, get away from busy airspace if you can. If you're supposed to be in controlled airspace, you can't stay there without comms. You need to exit that airspace or land as soon as practical at the nearest suitable aerodrome. Squawk 7600 on your transponder if you've got one — that tells radar that you've lost comms and can't respond. If you're in uncontrolled airspace, you can continue VFR, but you still need to land and get the radio fixed. You cannot legally continue flying indefinitely without a working radio. Get on the ground, find a technician, and get it sorted. The rules exist because a pilot without comms is a hazard to themselves and everyone else in the airspace. Your responsibility is to recognize the failure, take appropriate action, and get the aircraft down safely.
exam_tip: Complete radio failure: exit controlled airspace, squawk 7600, land at nearest aerodrome.
key_facts: Recognize genuine equipment failure after systematic checks
Squawk 7600 on transponder if equipped (indicates radio failure to radar)
Exit controlled airspace as soon as practical
In uncontrolled airspace, continue VFR and proceed to nearest aerodrome
Land and arrange radio repair; don't continue flying without comms
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Describe the checks that should be followed when becoming aware of an aircraft communication system failure, including a check of: Microphone or headset
concept: So you're in comms with ATC, everything's fine, and suddenly they disappear. They've lost their comms — it's not your radio. What do you do? First, monitor the frequency. ATC might come back on — they might be using backup equipment. Continue monitoring and listening for a minute. If they don't come back, try another frequency if you know it — maybe a different frequency for the same facility. If you've got no luck, try calling on 121.5. If ATC is off the air, other aircraft or Airways might know about it. The key is being patient and monitoring. ATS comms failures are rare but when they happen, everyone's trying to figure it out. Follow your last clearance if you were cleared for something specific — stay on that altitude or heading. Don't start deviating because you think they can't see you anymore. They might be monitoring on radar even if they're not transmitting. Eventually they'll come back on or you'll be in contact with another facility. Comms discipline and patience are your friends here.
exam_tip: ATS comms failure: monitor frequency, follow last clearance, try alternate freq or 121.5.
key_facts: ATS comms failure is rare but possible (backup equipment usually available)
Monitor frequency; ATS may return to service on backup
Follow your last clearance; maintain altitude and heading
Try alternate frequency for same facility if you know it
Try 121.5 MHz to alert other aircraft and Airways
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Detail the actions to be taken when experiencing loss of communications in:
concept: Loss of comms in different types of airspace requires different actions because the rules and procedures are different. In controlled airspace, you're supposed to be in contact with ATC, so losing comms is a more serious situation — you need specific procedures to protect yourself and other traffic. In uncontrolled airspace, you're already self-reliant, so losing comms is less of an immediate problem, but you still need to land and get it fixed. In special use airspace, you might have restrictions or requirements that your comms loss affects. The common thread is: recognize where you are, understand what the rules are for that airspace, and follow the procedures for comms loss in that specific situation. Don't just apply the same response everywhere — different airspace, different rules. This is why knowing your airspace structure matters. You need to understand controlled versus uncontrolled, understand where special use airspace is, and know the procedures for each. Then, if comms failure happens, you execute the right procedure for where you are.
exam_tip: Comms loss procedures differ: Controlled (more restricted), Uncontrolled (more flexible).
key_facts: Controlled airspace: follow last clearance, maintain level, squawk 7600, exit or land
Uncontrolled airspace: continue VFR, maintain awareness, land at nearest aerodrome
Special use airspace: depends on airspace type and specific requirements
Always know your current airspace type during flight
Procedures are designed to minimize conflict risk in each environment
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Detail the actions to be taken when experiencing loss of communications in: Controlled airspace
concept: You've lost comms in controlled airspace — you can't hear ATC and they can't hear you. This is serious because controlled airspace exists to manage traffic separation. Here's what you do: First, squawk 7600 on your transponder if you've got one. That tells radar that you're in comms failure. Second, follow your last clearance. If ATC cleared you to flight level 250, stay at 250. Don't start deviating because you think they can't see you — radar's still tracking you. Third, maintain the last heading you were given. Fourth, you've got 7 minutes to decide what to do. After 7 minutes at your current level, you can adjust your altitude if necessary to continue toward your destination, but you should be exiting controlled airspace or landing. Don't just wander around in the middle of controlled airspace with no comms. The entire point of these procedures is to keep you predictable so other traffic knows where you are. If you can exit the controlled airspace and continue to an uncontrolled aerodrome, do that. If you need to land in the controlled airspace, look for somewhere to put down and get on the ground.
exam_tip: Controlled airspace comms loss: squawk 7600, follow last clearance, maintain level 7 min, then adjust/exit.
key_facts: Squawk 7600 on transponder to indicate comms failure to radar
Follow your last clearance exactly (altitude, heading, routing)
Maintain your assigned level for 7 minutes after loss of comms
After 7 minutes, adjust altitude if necessary to continue toward destination
Exit controlled airspace or land as soon as practical
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Detail the actions to be taken when experiencing loss of communications in: Uncontrolled airspace
concept: You've lost comms in uncontrolled airspace. The difference is significant: you're already self-reliant in uncontrolled airspace, so comms loss is less of a regulatory problem. You can still navigate, you can still land, you're not dependent on ATC clearances. That said, you've still got a broken radio and you need to fix it. Squawk 7600 if you've got a transponder — it doesn't hurt and it alerts radar to your situation. Continue flying VFR toward the nearest suitable aerodrome. You maintain visual separation from other traffic — actually, you're doing that already in uncontrolled airspace. Get down to the nearest aerodrome and land. Once you're on the ground, you can call from the aerodrome to report that you've lost comms, get the radio fixed, and then continue. Flying around indefinitely without a working radio isn't acceptable, even in uncontrolled airspace. You're still supposed to have a radio. So the procedure is quick: recognize the failure, squawk 7600, proceed to nearest aerodrome VFR, land and fix it.
exam_tip: Uncontrolled airspace comms loss: squawk 7600, continue VFR, land at nearest aerodrome.
key_facts: Less restrictive than controlled airspace; you're already self-reliant
Squawk 7600 if transponder equipped
Continue VFR toward nearest suitable aerodrome
Maintain visual separation from other traffic
Land and arrange radio repair; radio must be operational
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Detail the actions to be taken when experiencing loss of communications in: Special use airspace
concept: Special use airspace — that could be military training areas, aerial work zones, or other restricted areas — has its own rules and you need to know them for your area. Comms loss in special use airspace depends on what type of airspace it is and what rules apply. If you're in a military training area, you might need to exit the area immediately. If you're in aerial work airspace, procedures might be different. The key is knowing your special use airspace before you fly. Check your AIPNZ, understand what airspace exists along your route, and know the rules for that airspace. Some special use airspace requires specific comms procedures or prior approval. If you lose comms in special use airspace, your first action is to exit it if safely practical, then follow standard comms loss procedures. Don't assume that special use airspace comms loss is handled the same way as controlled or uncontrolled airspace — it's not. Know the specific rules for your area before you fly through it.
exam_tip: Special use airspace: know the specific rules; exit if comms lost and it's practical.
key_facts: Special use airspace has specific procedures detailed in AIPNZ
Comms loss procedures depend on type of special use airspace
Exit special use airspace if safe and practical after comms loss
Follow standard comms failure procedures after exiting
Check AIPNZ before flying through special use airspace
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Describe corrective actions that could be taken, including: Change of frequency or station
concept: When you've lost comms, one of your first tries is changing frequency. If you're not hearing anyone on your current frequency, maybe something's wrong with the frequency selection or maybe the station's off the air. Try another frequency if you know one — for instance, if you've lost the tower, try approach on a different frequency. Or try 121.5 MHz, the universal emergency frequency — that's monitored widely and if anyone's going to hear you, 121.5 usually will. If you're in a controlled area and you know there's another approach or tower nearby, try that frequency. Sometimes a frequency change is all it takes — you might get through to someone on an adjacent frequency and they can relay your situation. The idea is systematic: try adjacent frequencies, try 121.5, try any alternate you know. Each attempt gives you a chance to get back in contact. Frequency changes are quick and easy, so it's one of your first corrective actions.
exam_tip: Comms loss: try alternate frequencies, try 121.5 MHz as universal emergency frequency.
key_facts: Try adjacent or alternate frequencies for same facility
121.5 MHz is monitored widely; good option if current frequency fails
Other nearby facilities might be able to hear and relay your situation
Frequency changes are quick corrective actions
Continue trying different frequencies until you establish contact
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Describe corrective actions that could be taken, including: Transmitting blind
concept: Transmitting blind means you're transmitting without knowing if anyone's hearing you. You can't hear them, but you're putting your message out there anyway. This is a valid corrective action when you've lost the ability to receive but you can still transmit — maybe your receiver's broken but the transmitter's working. Or maybe you're out of normal radio range but someone might pick up your transmission. You'd squawk 7600 and transmit your position, level, and intentions on your working frequency or on 121.5. You're essentially saying 'I can't hear you, but I'm transmitting my situation; if you can hear me, take note.' Other aircraft or ATS might hear you and relay the information. It's not ideal because there's no two-way communication, but it gets your information out. In some scenarios, transmitting blind is all you've got. You persist with position reports and information, hoping someone eventually hears you and relays it to someone who can help. Transmit blind is a tool, not a solution, but it's better than silence.
exam_tip: Transmitting blind: send position/level/intentions when receiver fails; others may relay.
key_facts: Transmit on working frequency or 121.5 with position, level, intentions
Squawk 7600 to alert radar to one-way communication
Other aircraft or ATS may hear and relay your information
Useful when receiver is broken but transmitter works
Continue position reports on regular intervals
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Describe corrective actions that could be taken, including: Increase in altitude
concept: You're having trouble raising a station on the radio. One reason could be range — you're too far away or too low. Increasing your altitude increases your radio range. Radio signals propagate better at altitude because you're above obstructions and you can 'see' further across the terrain. If you're struggling to reach a station and you've got altitude available, climbing a bit might help you get within range. It's a simple physics thing: higher equals further. Of course, if you're already at your assigned altitude or close to your destination, climbing might not help. And if you're in controlled airspace, you can't just start climbing without permission. But if you're in uncontrolled airspace and struggling with comms, gaining a bit of altitude is a reasonable corrective action. You might go from being 50 miles out of range to being 75 miles in range, which could be enough to establish communication. It's not a cure-all, but it's a tool in your troubleshooting kit.
exam_tip: Increase altitude to extend radio range; higher equals further.
key_facts: Higher altitude extends radio range and signal propagation
Climb increases signal line-of-sight to distant stations
Useful when out of normal radio range
Can only climb if you have altitude available and are not restricted
Altitude gain extends range but won't fix transmitter/receiver failure
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: Detail the speechless technique using unmodulated transmissions.
concept: The speechless technique is for situations where your transmitter's working but you can't speak — maybe your mic's broken, maybe there's an injury that prevents you from transmitting speech. You use the PTT button to transmit without voice: key clicks. One long key press means 'request priority landing.' Four key clicks means 'affirm' or 'yes.' Three key clicks means 'negative' or 'no.' Two key clicks means 'say again' or 'repeat.' ATC will catch on to what you're doing and can ask you simple yes/no questions that you can answer with key clicks. It's a workaround when speech isn't possible. You'll need to get ATC to figure out what's happening first — you might need to squawk 7600 or transmit blind with a written note — but once they understand you're using the speechless technique, they can manage a basic conversation with yes/no questions. It's not ideal and it's limiting, but it's better than complete communication loss. Train for this technique; understand the click patterns so you can use it if needed.
exam_tip: Speechless: 1 long click = priority landing; 4 clicks = yes; 3 = no; 2 = say again.
key_facts: For comms failures where transmitter works but voice unavailable
1 long click = request priority landing
4 clicks = affirm/yes
3 clicks = negative/no
2 clicks = say again/repeat
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of light signals used by ATC.
concept: If your radio's completely dead and you've ended up in the circuit at a controlled aerodrome, the tower can still communicate with you using light signals. These are standardized so that pilots who lose comms understand what the tower's trying to tell them. A steady green light in air means 'cleared to land.' A steady red light in air means 'give way and continue circuiting.' Green flashes in air mean 'return for landing,' which you might see if you're orbiting and the tower wants you back in the circuit. Red flashes in air mean 'aerodrome unsafe, do not land' — that's a serious warning. White flashes in air mean 'land at this aerodrome, await steady green.' If you're on the ground, steady green means 'cleared for takeoff,' and steady red means 'stop.' Green flashes on the ground mean 'cleared to taxi.' Red flashes on the ground mean 'taxi clear of the runway or landing area.' These signals are coordinated with light gun signals from the tower. Know these because if your radio fails on approach, you need to understand what the tower's trying to tell you with lights.
exam_tip: Light signals: Steady GREEN (land/takeoff), Steady RED (go-around/stop), flashes mean warnings/taxi.
key_facts: Steady GREEN in air = cleared to land; on ground = cleared for takeoff
Steady RED in air = give way/continue circuiting; on ground = stop
GREEN flashes in air = return for landing; on ground = cleared to taxi
RED flashes in air = aerodrome unsafe, do not land; on ground = clear the area
WHITE flashes in air = land here, await steady green
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

syllabus_desc: State the occasions when TIBA (traffic information broadcasts by aircraft) might be introduced.
concept: TIBA stands for Traffic Information Broadcasts by Aircraft, and it's a system for when the normal ATS is not available in certain airspace. Imagine you're flying in an area that doesn't have radar or ATC coverage — that happens in some parts of New Zealand, particularly in remote or lower-traffic areas. TIBA allows pilots to broadcast their position and intentions to each other on designated frequencies, building a shared traffic picture without a formal ATS facility. TIBA is introduced in areas designated in the AIPNZ where ATS is intermittent or unavailable. It's your way of letting other traffic in the area know where you are. You broadcast at regular intervals — usually every 10-15 minutes or at reporting points — with your position, altitude, and routing. It's essentially pilot-to-pilot sharing of the traffic picture. TIBA uses standard phraseology: 'ALL STATIONS [callsign], position, level, speed, intentions.' It's a collaborative system where everyone's doing their bit to make the airspace safer. If you're flying in an area with TIBA, you need to know the procedure and use it.
exam_tip: TIBA: pilot broadcasts of position/level/intentions when ATS unavailable in designated areas.
key_facts: TIBA used in designated areas where ATS is unavailable or intermittent
Pilots broadcast to build shared traffic picture
Broadcast format: 'ALL STATIONS [callsign], position, level, speed, intentions'
Broadcasts at regular intervals (typically 10-15 minutes or at reporting points)
Requires designation in AIPNZ ENR 1.15
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

topic: 2.18 — Loss of Communications — ATS Equipment Failure
syllabus_desc: Detail the TIBA procedures with respect to: VHF frequencies to be used
concept: TIBA broadcasts happen on specific designated VHF frequencies published in the AIPNZ. You don't just pick a random frequency; it's specified for each TIBA area. The frequency is designed so that pilots in that area can all hear each other on the same channel. Before you fly into a TIBA area, check the AIPNZ to find the designated frequency. It might be the standard approach frequency for that region, or it might be a specific TIBA frequency. Once you've identified the frequency, you'll monitor it and make your position broadcasts on schedule. The frequency is your lifeline to other traffic in that area, so tune it early and keep monitoring. If you're heading through multiple TIBA areas, make sure you've got all the frequencies written down before you depart. Don't try to find frequencies during the flight. Plan your frequencies as part of your pre-flight prep, just like you do with any other navigation or communication planning.
exam_tip: TIBA frequency: check AIPNZ ENR 1.15 for designated VHF frequency for each area.
key_facts: TIBA frequencies are designated and published in AIPNZ ENR 1.15
Different areas may have different frequencies
All pilots in TIBA area use same frequency to build traffic picture
Frequencies are specific to geographic areas; write them down pre-flight
Monitor designated frequency continuously when in TIBA area
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

topic: 2.18 — Loss of Communications — ATS Equipment Failure
syllabus_desc: Detail the TIBA procedures with respect to: Listening watch
concept: When you're in TIBA airspace, you need to maintain a listening watch on the designated TIBA frequency. That means you're actively monitoring the frequency, not just occasionally glancing at it. You need to hear other aircraft broadcasting their positions so you can build a mental picture of where traffic is. Equally, other aircraft need to hear your broadcasts. A listening watch is a professional radio discipline habit. You don't switch away from the TIBA frequency to chat on a different frequency or for other non-essential reasons. You keep that frequency active and you listen. If you're monitoring two frequencies (maybe a TIBA frequency and a regional frequency), you're giving priority attention to the TIBA frequency because it's your lifeline in an ATS-less area. The point of TIBA is collaborative traffic picture building, and you can't be part of that picture if you're not listening. Take the listening watch seriously — it's your primary tool for traffic awareness in TIBA areas.
exam_tip: Listening watch: actively monitor TIBA frequency; hear all traffic broadcasts.
key_facts: Maintain active listening watch on TIBA frequency when in designated area
Other aircraft rely on you hearing and understanding their broadcasts
Build mental traffic picture from position reports you hear
Don't switch away from TIBA frequency for non-essential reasons
Listening watch is fundamental to TIBA safety
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking

topic: 2.18 — Loss of Communications — ATS Equipment Failure
syllabus_desc: Detail the TIBA procedures with respect to: Times of broadcasts
concept: TIBA broadcasts have specified times — you don't broadcast whenever you feel like it. Typically, you broadcast every 10 to 15 minutes or at designated reporting points. The exact timing will be specified in the AIPNZ for each TIBA area. The reason for fixed timing is so that all pilots in the area can predict when they'll hear from each other. If you broadcast on an unpredictable schedule, the traffic picture becomes chaotic. At set intervals, everyone in the area makes a broadcast, and all pilots build a coherent picture of the traffic. If you're at a reporting point — a geographic location or timing point on your route — you might broadcast at that point even if it's not exactly on the regular schedule. The key is coordination: you broadcast at regular intervals so other pilots can count on regular position updates from you. This is professional radio discipline. Check the AIPNZ for the specific broadcast intervals and reporting points for your TIBA area, and stick to that schedule.
exam_tip: TIBA broadcasts: every 10-15 minutes or at reporting points per AIPNZ.
key_facts: TIBA broadcasts at regular intervals (typically 10-15 minutes)
Additional broadcasts at designated reporting points
Regular timing allows other pilots to predict position reports
Check AIPNZ for specific broadcast intervals and reporting points
Professional discipline: broadcast on schedule reliably
source: AC 91-9/AC172-1 Rev 16, CAA GAP Plane Talking
